about: Machine-readable blurbs for the DOMY symbolic cosmology. The narrative canon lives in SYMBOLISM.md; these one-to-three-sentence texts are the extended info the app may surface (hovers, info panels, docs).

philosophy.white_light: White light is the sum of all colors. Sunday sits in the center because the Sun is not one color among six - the six arms are sunlight decomposed, the star is the prism, Sunday is the source.
philosophy.paint_and_light: Before Newton the world knew color as pigment: yellow, red and blue, mixed into orange, green and purple - the painter's wheel of six. Newton's prism opened the second world: red, green and blue light with cyan, magenta and yellow between them. DOMY's 'paint' and 'light' palette styles are exactly these two worlds.
philosophy.gold_and_silver: Alchemy gives one metal to each luminary: gold to the Sun, silver to the Moon. On the dial the top is solar noon and the bottom is midnight, so gold crowns the top tip and silver the bottom - the day metal above, the night metal below.
philosophy.cross_cure: Every body carries a bright and a dark face - a virtue and a vice. Across each axis of the star, one body's virtue is the cure for the opposite body's vice.
philosophy.professions: Medieval astrology drew the 'children of the planets': each planet rules the callings born under it. The seven archetypal professions of the old world land one per body, and each snaps onto its body's virtue and vice.
philosophy.theological_trio: Faith, Hope and Love are a separate set from the seven virtue/vice pairs (Love alone belongs to both, as in tradition). They span three kinds of places: Faith at the center (Sunday, toward God - the vertical), Hope on the dawn arm (west, toward what comes - the horizon), Love on Venus' arm (toward people - the human).
philosophy.religion_alternates: The owner's art holds fourteen religions in two full sets. The basic seven sit on the arms (Freemasonry on Tuesday, owner decision); the alternate seven each take the day they fit best: Ancient Egypt on Sunday (Ra reborn each dawn), Druidism on Monday (lunar reckoning, groves and tides), Zoroastrianism on Tuesday (the fire Atar, asha as the courage of truth), Shamanism on Wednesday (the walker between worlds on the messenger's day), Sikhism on Thursday (Guruvar taken literally - ten Gurus, langar generosity), Babylon on Friday (Ishtar IS Venus; Babylon invented the planetary week), Voodoo on Saturday (Baron Samedi, 'Baron Saturday', keeper of the crossroads).

blurbs.day: Sunday is the source, not one day among six: as white light contains every color, the week's center contains every day.
blurbs.profession: The Sun is the royal planet, and its child is the Ruler - whose lesson is Humility and whose sin is Pride.
blurbs.greek: Helios drives the sun chariot across the sky each day; the Greeks named this day hemera Heliou, the day of the Sun.
blurbs.norse: Sol (Sunna) is the Norse sun goddess, chased across the sky by the wolf Skoll; sunnudagr is her day.
blurbs.religion: Christianity worships on the Lord's Day; in 321 Constantine made the 'day of the Sun' the day of rest, fusing Sun and Church in history. Sunday is also the day of Faith - with Hope at dawn and Love on Venus, the three theological virtues span the dial.
blurbs.color: White-gold: the sum of all colors and the Sun's own metal.
blurbs.virtue_vice: Pride is the self at the center of everything; Humility is its only cure. The center heals itself.
blurbs.religion_alt: Rome's soldier-mysteries crowned the Unconquered Sun: seven grades of initiation under the seven planets, dies Solis for their holy day — the same Sunday this dial keeps at its white-gold center.
blurbs.egypt: Ra is the sun itself, not its god: sailing the day barque from horizon to horizon, dying into the Duat each night and reborn at every dawn. Sunday's white-gold center is his oldest throne — no pantheon fused sun, king and temple more completely.
blurbs.slavic: Dažbog is the giving sun whose grandchildren the Slavs called themselves — he pours out grain, gold and warmth from the center and keeps nothing back.
blurbs.alchemy: Gold, the sun made solid, never rusts and never tarnishes — the incorruptible metal that is the goal of the whole Great Work.

blurbs.day: Monday is the Moon's day in every tongue that kept the planetary week - Selene's day in Greek, Mani's in Norse, Somvar in Sanskrit.
blurbs.profession: Medieval medicine followed the Moon - bloodletting calendars ran on its phases - and the physician keeps the night watch at the sickbed: a calm hand against the patient's fear. (The Moon's traditional alternate child is the Sailor, ruler of tides.)
blurbs.greek: Selene is the Titan goddess of the Moon, crossing the night sky in a silver chariot drawn by white horses.
blurbs.norse: Mani steers the Moon through the night, hunted by the wolf Hati until the world's end.
blurbs.religion: The Buddhist calendar is lunar: Uposatha observance days follow the Moon's phases, and Vesak - the Buddha's birth, awakening and passing - falls on a full moon.
blurbs.color: Blue is the still hour before dawn: moonlight on a sleeping world.
blurbs.virtue_vice: The Moon's bright face is Serenity, its dark face Fear - the night brings both peace and dread. Courage, from the opposite arm, is Fear's cure.
blurbs.religion_alt: The druids reckoned time by the Moon - groves, tides and the lunar count of nights; the serenity of the sacred wood, and the fear of its dark.
blurbs.egypt: Khonsu, "the traveller", is the moon crossing the night sky — Thebes' young healer and reckoner of months, wrapped like the dead yet carrying the regalia of life; the serenity of the crossing, and the old fear of the dark it passes through.
blurbs.slavic: Hors is the pale light of the night, the moon-disc whose path the werewolf-prince Vseslav crossed as he ran through the dark before dawn.
blurbs.alchemy: Silver mirrors the light it is given and blackens in the air, keeping the Moon's own habit of waxing bright and waning dark.

blurbs.day: Tuesday belongs to the red planet: Ares' day to the Greeks, Tyr's day (tysdagr) to the Norse, Mangalvar in the Vedic week.
blurbs.profession: Mars' child is the Soldier, whose two faces are the body's virtue and vice themselves: Courage in defense, Wrath unleashed.
blurbs.greek: Ares is the storm of battle itself - the Greeks honored his ferocity and feared his rage in the same breath.
blurbs.norse: Tyr, god of war AND justice, gave his right hand to bind the wolf Fenrir - courage in the service of order.
blurbs.religion: Freemasonry is sworn on oaths - and Tyr, this arm's god, gave his hand to seal one. The square and compasses build the inner temple with a soldier's discipline: Courage set to work, Wrath bound by ritual.
blurbs.color: Orange is the working afternoon: the ember of the day's fire.
blurbs.virtue_vice: Mars carries both Courage and Wrath - the same fire that defends can destroy. Serenity, from the Moon's arm, quells Wrath; Courage cures the Moon's Fear.
blurbs.religion_alt: Zoroastrianism keeps the sacred fire Atar burning, and its heart is asha - truth and justice - the courage of the flame against the lie.
blurbs.egypt: Montu is the raised arm of Egypt's armies — the falcon-headed war god of Thebes whose name was the highest praise a fighting pharaoh could earn: Courage under the king's command, Wrath the same blade turned loose.
blurbs.slavic: Svetovid is the four-faced war-god of Arkona whose sacred white horse, led over crossed spears, foretold whether the campaign would be blessed.
blurbs.alchemy: Iron is the soldier's metal and the iron in our blood — Mars really does run, red and breath by breath, in every vein.

blurbs.day: Wednesday is Mercury's day - Hermes' day in Greek, Woden's (Odin's) day in the north, Budhvar in Sanskrit, the water-star day in East Asia.
blurbs.profession: Hermes is the god of roads and trade, and his child is the Merchant - who weighs with Wisdom and falls to Greed.
blurbs.greek: Hermes is the messenger between worlds: god of roads, merchants, thieves and cunning speech, guide of souls into the underworld.
blurbs.norse: Odin hung nine nights on the world-tree and gave an eye at Mimir's well - all of it for wisdom. Wednesday is literally Wodnesdaeg, his day.
blurbs.religion: In the East Asian week Wednesday is the day of the water star - and water is the Tao's central image: the soft thing that overcomes the hard, the way that flows without striving.
blurbs.color: Purple is midnight: the mourning color, the day's grave and its deepest mystery.
blurbs.virtue_vice: Mercury's bright face is Wisdom, its dark face Greed - the merchant god counts both coins and stars. Generosity, from Jupiter's arm, cures Greed; Wisdom curbs Jupiter's Excess.
blurbs.religion_alt: The shaman walks between worlds on the messenger's day - Odin hanging on the tree for wisdom is itself a shamanic initiation.
blurbs.egypt: Thoth is the ibis-headed scribe of the gods — inventor of writing, reckoner of years and moons, recorder of the verdict when the heart meets Ma'at's feather; Hermes' elder mirror on the messenger's midnight arm.
blurbs.slavic: Veles is the horned cattle-god and serpent at the world-tree's roots, sworn beside Perun in the treaties and grandsire of the bard Boyan.
blurbs.alchemy: Quicksilver is the only metal liquid at room temperature, a mirror you can pour — Mercurius, the messenger who carries every metal from one state to another.

blurbs.day: Thursday is the king planet's day: Zeus' day in Greek, Thor's day in the north, Guruvar - the guru's day - in the Vedic week.
blurbs.profession: Jupiter's children are priests, judges and teachers - Brihaspati IS the guru of the gods, and Thursday is the teacher's day: generous of hand, jovial of table.
blurbs.greek: Zeus rules sky and thunder from Olympus, king of gods and keeper of oaths and guests.
blurbs.norse: Thor, thunder itself, guards gods and men with the hammer Mjolnir; thorsdagr became Thursday.
blurbs.religion: In Hindu tradition Thursday IS Brihaspativar: Brihaspati, guru of the gods, is the planet Jupiter itself, and his day is the week's most devotional.
blurbs.color: Yellow is noon at the top of the dial - and 'jovial', joyful, literally means 'of Jupiter'.
blurbs.virtue_vice: Jupiter's bright face is Generosity - the open royal hand; its dark face is Excess, the jovial table overflowing. Wisdom, from Mercury's arm, curbs Excess; Generosity cures Greed.
blurbs.religion_alt: Sikhism takes Guruvar literally - a faith of ten Gurus whose daily practice is langar, generosity served at a common table.
blurbs.egypt: Amun is the Hidden One — king of the gods, joined with the sun as Amun-Ra, the god the Greeks themselves named Zeus Ammon; Thursday's crown arm wears his twin plumes.
blurbs.slavic: Perun is the oak-crowned thunder-god whose six-petalled storm-mark the clock's hexagram echoes, and whose rain, wrung from the serpent, is the sky's generosity.
blurbs.alchemy: Tin makes bells and bronze ring, and cries out with the faint 'tin cry' when you bend it — Jupiter's benevolent, sounding metal.

blurbs.day: Friday is the day of the evening star: Aphrodite's day in Greek, Freya's day in the north, Shukravar in Sanskrit.
blurbs.profession: Venus' children in the old woodcuts are musicians, painters and lovers - the Artist, who works from Love and burns with Jealousy.
blurbs.greek: Aphrodite, born of sea-foam, rules love, beauty and desire - the planet Venus is her lamp at dusk.
blurbs.norse: Freya, goddess of love and of the slain, rides a chariot drawn by cats and weeps tears of gold; frjadagr is her day.
blurbs.religion: Islam gathers on Friday: Jumu'ah, the congregational prayer, makes this the blessed day of the week.
blurbs.color: Red is the evening: the sunset's blood-glow, the hour when Venus rises.
blurbs.virtue_vice: Venus carries Love and its shadow Jealousy - the fear of losing what is loved. Patience, from Saturn's arm, heals Jealousy; Love dissolves Saturn's Envy.
blurbs.religion_alt: Babylon named the planets gods and gave this clock its seven-day week; Ishtar IS Venus - the evening star, love and war in one body.
blurbs.egypt: Hathor - lady of love, music, beauty and joy, the Lady of the West who receives the setting sun into her country; the Greeks called her Aphrodite, and Venus' red evening is her hour.
blurbs.slavic: Mokoš is the great spinning goddess and Moist Mother Earth, the lone goddess of Kyiv's pantheon, who lived on as St Paraskeva venerated every Friday.
blurbs.alchemy: Copper is aes cyprium, the metal of Venus' island of Cyprus, and her glyph ♀ is the very hand-mirror it was first polished into.

blurbs.day: Saturday is Saturn's day - Cronus' day to the Greeks, Shanivar in Sanskrit. The Norse alone gave it no god: laugardagr, washing day; the Sabbath stands there instead.
blurbs.profession: Cronus carries the harvest sickle, and Saturn's child is the Farmer - who sows and WAITS (Patience) and eyes the neighbor's field (Envy).
blurbs.greek: Cronus, Titan of time and the harvest, devoured his own children out of envy and fear of being replaced - until Zeus, hidden away, returned to overthrow him.
blurbs.norse: Loki stands on Saturday (owner canon): the bound trickster, chained beneath the serpent venom as Cronus was cast down - the north gave its rest day to the god who never rests. His envy of Baldr brought the brightest god down.
blurbs.religion: Shabbat is Judaism's seventh day of rest - and in Hebrew the planet Saturn is named Shabtai, after the Sabbath itself: the day named the planet.
blurbs.color: Green is the morning: dew, growth, the week renewing itself on the day of rest.
blurbs.virtue_vice: Saturn, keeper of time, teaches Patience; his shadow is Envy, the bitter green of wanting what another has. Love, from Venus' arm, dissolves Envy; Patience heals Jealousy.
blurbs.religion_alt: Voodoo's keeper of the crossroads is Baron Samedi - Baron Saturday by name - the patience of the grave and the envy of the living.
blurbs.egypt: Osiris is the harvest that dies and returns — lord of the underworld and of rebirth, green-skinned like sprouting grain on Saturn's green morning; the patience of the buried seed, with Set's envy the vice he answered from the grave.
blurbs.slavic: Morana is the goddess of winter and death whose straw effigy is drowned every spring equinox — death as the hinge that lets the year be born again.
blurbs.alchemy: Lead is the heaviest, slowest metal, the prima materia the Work begins from — the base matter alchemy promises to turn into gold.

octa_extras.blurb: The west arm sits on 06h - sunrise. Cyan is the pale blue-green sky before the sun clears the horizon, and its mood is Hope: the emotion that looks forward.
The east arm sits on 18h - sunset. Red-orange is the burning evening sky, and its mood is Longing: the emotion that looks back. Hope and Longing face each other across the dial as dawn faces dusk.

cures: Humility cures Pride - the center heals itself.

articles_note: articles[theme][body] = {base, variants}: base = 3-4 left-aligned paragraphs (blank-line separated) weaving the entity with its day, color, hour mood, virtue and vice, anchored in the owner's image; variants = one short paragraph per pointer/palette combination (hexa_paint, hexa_light, octa_paint, octa_light, cross, trio). religion = the basic seven (Freemasonry on Tuesday, owner decision); religion_alt = the alternates by body (Druidism Mon, Zoroastrianism Tue, Shamanism Wed, Sikhism Thu, Voodoo Sat; Sunday and Friday have no alternate yet), entries carry a 'name' field. Display location TBD by the owner.

sun.base:
The Sun is the only body on this dial that burns with its own fire, and the image hides nothing of it: a seething sphere of white-gold plasma, its surface boiling in granular cells of yellow and orange, ringed by a thin fringe of flame-red corona. Everything else on the wheel — every planet, and the Moon most of all — merely returns a borrowed portion of this light.

That is why, on the hexa paint dial, the Sun takes no arm at all: it holds the center, and its color is white-gold — the sum of every color. The six arms are sunlight decomposed; the star itself is the prism; Sunday is the source. To pin the Sun to one hue would be to mistake a part for the whole.

Its metal is gold, the day metal that crowns the dial's top tip at noon, and its calling is the Ruler — for the royal planet carries the royal pair of faces: Humility as virtue, Pride as vice. This is the one axis that does not reach across the dial: the center's cure lives in the center. Humility cures Pride; the source heals itself, or nothing does.

And so Sunday rules no single hour but all of them: joy, zeal, passion, sorrow, calm and renewal all radiate from the middle, the way the day's every mood is finally some angle of the same light.

variants.hexa_paint: The home position: the center of the dial, white-gold, the source from which all six arms take their color. No hour belongs to it because every hour does.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette nothing moves — the center stays the center, white-gold. If anything the symbol grows more literal: this is Newton's own white beam, caught one instant before the prism splits it into red, green and blue.
variants.octa_paint: On the octa pointer the Sun steps out of the center for the first time and takes the top arm at 12h in yellow (#F8E600) — Joy, the day's peak. The center's white stays behind at the hub; stepping into the wheel, the sun puts on the color of its own noon.
variants.octa_light: At 12h on the octa light wheel the Sun wears green — for in the prism's arrangement green is the light that sits at noon. The source submits to its own spectrum and takes the middle color of the rainbow it makes.
variants.cross: On the cross the arms point at the year's hinges, and the Sun holds the top arm at 12h, yellow for Summer, sharing it with Jupiter. The summer solstice is the Sun's own festival — the day it stands highest, the noon of the year.
variants.trio: The Trinity returns the Sun to the center, white-gold, with only three arms around it — Faith's yellow, Love's red, Hope's blue: the painter's three primaries. The prism reading at its simplest — white light decomposed not into six moods but into the three virtues, and Sunday still the source of them all.

moon.base:
The Moon in the owner's image is full and silver-gray: dark seas pool across its upper face, pale cratered highlands fill the south, and near the bottom rim one bright crater throws its rays outward like a star scratched into stone. It is the most honest portrait on the dial — a world of ash and dust that shines only because the Sun is somewhere behind you.

Its home on the hexa paint dial is the arm at 4h, deep blue, the hour before dawn — Calm. Blue is the still color, the moonlit color, the temperature of a world asleep; and 4h is the night's last watch, when darkness is at its most complete and its most peaceful at once.

That doubleness is the Moon's whole nature. Monday's body carries Serenity as its virtue and Fear as its vice — the same unlit world, once accepted and once dreaded. Its healer hangs straight across the dial: Mars in the working afternoon, whose Courage cures Fear, while the Moon's Serenity quells Mars' Wrath in return. The Physician is its calling — the calm hand at the night sickbed against the patient's fear — and silver its metal, the night's answer to the Sun's gold, kept at the dial's bottom tip.

Calm rules 4h because that is the hour serenity is actually tested: nothing left of yesterday, nothing yet of tomorrow, only borrowed silver light. Whoever is at peace then is at peace.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm: 4h, deep pigment blue, Calm — the Moon presiding over the hour before dawn.
variants.hexa_light: The light palette keeps the Moon at 4h but tunes its blue to the prism's own primary — a purer, electric blue. Calm read not as painted stillness but as the actual color of pre-dawn light.
variants.octa_paint: With eight arms the wheel redistributes, and the Moon slides an hour deeper into the dark: the arm at 3h, a colder sea-blue, the deep night. Farther from dawn, closer to the night's true middle — the Moon more at home, if anything.
variants.octa_light: At 3h in light colors the arm turns pure blue (#0000FF) — the deep night at its own full voltage, the primary beam of the dark hours. The Moon's calm here is at its deepest and most certain.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons palette, and the Moon takes the 6h dawn arm, green for Spring, sharing it with Saturn. The spring equinox is the year's own daybreak — a fitting post for the body that ends every night.
variants.trio: The trio keeps the Moon at its own 4h, but the arm now reads Hope — blue, shared with Mercury — and its tip is now the very center of its hue: it guards the morning third from 0h to 8h. Fitting work for the body that ends every night: hope is the Moon's calm turned to face the coming sun.

mars.base:
Mars is the rust-colored one, and the owner's image shows the detail that matters: a vast dark scar torn across its equator, a canyon system long enough to stretch across a continent. The planet named for war actually looks wounded — its red is the red of iron oxide, of dried blood, of an old battlefield.

Yet on the hexa paint dial its arm at 16h is orange, not red — because the arm colors belong to the hours, and 16h is the working afternoon, the mood of Zeal. Orange is the ember-color of effort: past noon's peak but far from evening, the hours when fire is not display but fuel. Mars, the planet of drive, is exactly the body to stoke them.

Tuesday's planet carries one fire with two uses: Courage, the flame that defends, and Wrath, the same flame let loose on its own side. The Soldier is its profession because a soldier is precisely this pair walking. And its remedy hangs straight across the dial at 4h: the Moon's Serenity quells Wrath, while Mars sends Courage back along the axis to cure the Moon's Fear — the warrior and the night-watch healing each other.

Zeal rules the afternoon because that is when work is won or lost: morning's freshness is spent, evening's rest not yet earned, and only the stubborn ember carries a task to its end.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm: 16h, ember orange, Zeal — Mars stoking the working afternoon.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette Mars stays at 16h but the ember flares to pure yellow — pigment's orange has no seat in the prism wheel, so the afternoon fire burns at light's brightest hue instead. Zeal, only hotter.
variants.octa_paint: The octa wheel moves Mars an hour toward noon: the arm at 15h, amber (#ECB800), the mid-afternoon. The fight begins earlier — zeal borrowing some of noon's gold without leaving its own fire.
variants.octa_light: At 15h in light colors Mars wears a harder, brassier yellow — the mid-afternoon read as metal rather than flame, effort with the shine worn off.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Mars takes the 18h dusk arm, red for Autumn, and finally wears its own color — sharing the arm with Venus, as Ares shared everything with Aphrodite. War and love on one arm, pointing at the autumn equinox, the year's long evening.
variants.trio: The Trinity moves Mars to 20h, onto Love's red arm beside Venus — each arm's tip centers its third of the day, and this pair guards the evening from 16h to 24h. Mars finally wears its own red, and Love reads through it as protection: courage keeping watch so that what is loved can sleep.

mercury.base:
The owner's Mercury is a small cratered ball painted in two minds: one face warm amber-tan, the other pale ashen gray, the whole surface pocked with craters like a well-traveled road. It suits the planet perfectly — the innermost wanderer and the fastest of all — it circles the Sun in 88 days — a slow-turning world of the harshest extremes, its days scorching and its nights freezing on the very same plain.

Its home on the hexa paint dial is the bottom arm at 0h, purple, midnight — the mood of Sorrow. Purple is the mixed color, red mourning dissolved in blue stillness, and midnight is the mind's hour: the day is dead, tomorrow unborn, and thought runs on alone. Where better for the planet of the mind than the hour when only the mind is awake?

Wednesday's planet carries Wisdom as its virtue and Greed as its vice — the merchant's pair, for the same cleverness that understands the world can start counting it instead. Hermes was god of merchants and of roads, and his cure comes down the longest road on the dial: Jupiter's Generosity, straight above at noon, cures Greed, while Mercury's Wisdom travels back up the axis to curb Jupiter's Excess.

So Sorrow rules midnight not as despair but as depth: the hour of accounting, when the mind weighs the day honestly because no one is watching. Mercury, who guided souls across the last border, keeps that hour.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm: the dial's bottom at 0h, mourning purple, Sorrow — Mercury keeping the midnight watch.
variants.hexa_light: The light palette keeps Mercury at 0h but turns its purple to magenta — the one color missing from the rainbow, existing only where red and blue light meet. A color the mind must invent: no hue could suit Mercury better.
variants.octa_paint: The octa wheel pulls Mercury off midnight to the arm at 21h, deep red, the late evening — the hour of last errands and closing accounts, before the day's books are shut.
variants.octa_light: At 21h in light colors the red sharpens to a pure signal red — the late evening as a filament still glowing, the mind refusing to switch off.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Mercury returns to the bottom arm at 0h, blue for Winter, and holds it alone — the only body that shares its arm with no one. The winter solstice is the year's midnight, and Mercury stands that watch solitary, as thought does.
variants.trio: The trio lifts Mercury off midnight to 4h, onto Hope's blue arm beside the Moon, guarding the morning third from 0h to 8h. Hope is the theological shape of Wisdom's forward look: the mind that weighed the day at midnight now stands at the morning's gate, counting what comes instead of what has gone.

jupiter.base:
Jupiter is the giant in banded robes: the owner's image shows cream and caramel stripes wrapping the whole globe, whipped into curls at their borders, and low on one side the Great Red Spot — a single storm wider than the Earth that has been turning for centuries. Everything about the planet is surplus: the largest body, the loudest weather, a king's wardrobe of clouds.

On the hexa paint dial the king takes the crown position: the top arm at 12h, yellow, noon — the mood of Joy. This is no metaphor smuggled in afterward: 'jovial' literally means 'of Jupiter,' and the top of this dial is where the star's vertex points at true solar noon. The day's peak belongs to the planet of abundance.

Thursday's planet gives with an open hand — Generosity is its virtue — and keeps giving past the point of sense, for Excess is its vice: the royal table overflowing. Its calling is the Priest and Teacher, the one who distributes what he has received; and its correction hangs at the far end of the dial's spine — Mercury's Wisdom at midnight curbs Excess, while Jupiter's Generosity pours down the same axis to cure Mercury's Greed. The full hand and the counting hand, balancing each other.

Joy rules noon because noon is the day's own generosity: the most light, the most warmth, the moment the world is given more than it asked for. Jupiter simply makes that hour a person.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm: the dial's top at 12h, noon yellow, Joy — the king planet at the crown of the star.
variants.hexa_light: The light palette keeps Jupiter at the 12h crown but dresses it in green — the middle color of the spectrum, which the prism wheel seats at noon. Joy read as fullness of light rather than gold.
variants.octa_paint: On the octa wheel the Sun steps out of the center and takes the crown, so Jupiter yields the top and moves to 9h, green, the mid-morning — the teacher's hour, when the day's work of giving actually begins. A king gracious enough to make room for the source itself.
variants.octa_light: At 9h in light colors Jupiter wears cyan — the mid-morning as clear sky. Generosity in its freshest register, before the heat of the day.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Jupiter keeps the top arm at 12h, yellow for Summer, sharing it with the Sun. The summer solstice is the year at its most generous — the longest light, the fullest table — and the two most royal bodies preside over it together.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Jupiter keeps its crown: the 12h arm, yellow — but the yellow is Faith's now, shared with Saturn, and the arm centers the day's third - 8h to 16h, noon at its tip. Faith reads naturally through the king planet: generosity is faith practiced with the hands, giving before any return is proven.

venus.base:
The owner's Venus is not the pale pearl of the evening sky but the truth beneath it: a globe of molten orange and red, its surface veined with bright fire like embers seen through smoke. That is the real planet under the lovely clouds — a furnace hotter than Mercury — and it is the perfect portrait of the passion it stands for: the most beautiful light in the sky, burning at close range.

Its home on the hexa paint dial is the arm at 20h, deep red, the evening — the mood of Passion. The placement is astronomy before it is poetry: Venus is the evening star, the first light to open over the sunset, and 20h is when the sky itself turns blood-red. Friday's planet rules the hours when work ends and wanting begins.

Love is its virtue and Jealousy its vice — not love's opposite but love's shadow, the fear of losing what is loved. Its cure stands straight across the dial in the green morning: Saturn's Patience heals Jealousy, while Venus' Love flows back across the axis to dissolve Saturn's Envy. The Artist is Venus' calling — the old woodcuts drew the planet's children as musicians and lovers — and art is exactly this: passion given patience enough to become form.

Passion rules the evening because evening is when the day stops demanding and starts desiring: the light turns red, the work lets go, and what remains is what one actually loves.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm: 20h, blood red, Passion — the evening star over the evening hour.
variants.hexa_light: The light palette keeps Venus at 20h and gives its red the prism's full voltage — pure red, light's own primary. Passion undiluted.
variants.octa_paint: The octa wheel moves Venus onto the 18h dusk arm itself, glowing orange — the mood of Longing, the emotion that looks back. The evening star exactly at sunset, at the moment the day becomes something to miss.
variants.octa_light: At 18h in light colors Venus burns sunset-orange, the sky's own dusk hue. Longing as the west going down in fire.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Venus holds the 18h arm, red for Autumn, sharing it with Mars — Aphrodite and Ares together, as in the oldest gossip of the gods. The autumn equinox is the year's dusk: the harvest of what was loved, and the first cold that makes one hold it tighter.
variants.trio: Venus does not move at all: the Trinity's red Love arm is her own 20h, and Love is already her virtue — the one word belonging to both the seven pairs and the theological three. Mars joins her there, and together they guard the evening from 16h to 24h: passion and courage guarding the dark third of the day.

saturn.base:
Saturn is the only body on the dial that carries its own circle: the owner's image shows the butter-gold globe tilted inside its broad rings, band upon band of cream and caramel, with the dark divisions drawn clean between them. A planet wearing a halo of orbiting dust — or, seen with this dial's eyes, a planet wearing the year itself as a ring.

On the hexa paint dial it takes the arm at 8h, green, the morning — the mood of Renewal. It seems a paradox to give the slowest wanderer the freshest hour, but that is the point: Saturn takes nearly thirty years to close one circle, so no body knows better that mornings return. Green is dew and growth, and Saturday's morning is the week's own renewal — the day of rest, whose planet in Hebrew is named Shabtai, after the Sabbath itself.

Patience is its virtue, learned from its own pace, and Envy its vice — the bitter green of another's harvest, the oldest failure of those who wait. The Farmer is its calling: Cronus carries the harvest sickle, and farming is patience practiced in public — sow, then wait, and do not stare too long at the neighbor's field. The cure stands across the dial in the red evening: Venus' Love dissolves Envy, while Saturn's Patience travels back to heal Venus' Jealousy — the two slow arts, waiting and loving, mending each other.

Renewal rules 8h because the morning is where patience pays out: whatever was buried in the dark comes up green. The week's day of rest owns that hour on behalf of all the others.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm: 8h, harvest green, Renewal — the slow planet keeping the morning of the day of rest.
variants.hexa_light: The light palette keeps Saturn at 8h but cools its green to cyan — morning read as sky rather than field, the pale clean light after sunrise.
variants.octa_paint: The octa wheel moves Saturn onto the 6h dawn arm itself, cyan, the mood of Hope. It is the most philosophical move on the wheel: hope is simply patience turned to face forward, and the slowest planet earns the arm that waits for the Sun.
variants.octa_light: At 6h in light colors Saturn wears the deep blue of the last minutes before sunrise — Hope at its coldest and most certain, the hour the farmer already stands in the field.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Saturn holds the 6h arm, green for Spring, sharing it with the Moon. The spring equinox is sowing time — the farmer's own festival — patience at the exact moment it commits its seed to the ground.
variants.trio: The Trinity promotes Saturn from the morning to the crown: the 12h Faith arm, yellow, shared with Jupiter — the two slow giants on one arm, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. Faith reads through Saturn as patience aimed upward: trusting a harvest that takes thirty years to come round.

sun.base:
Helios is not a god who carries the sun; he is the sun, the Titan the Greeks trusted to see everything done under the open sky — witnesses swore their oaths by him because nothing escapes the one who rides above it all. In the drawing he drives the quadriga, four winged horses hauling his chariot through a dark, star-scattered heaven; a spiked disc of gold blazes behind his head like a halo too heavy for any crown, and in his raised hand he grips a staff tipped with a small burning sun. He does not point at his element — he wields it.

On the six-armed dial he takes no arm at all: Sunday sits in the center, colored white-gold. This is the founding sentence of the whole design — white light is the sum of all colors, so the six arm-hues around him are nothing but his own light decomposed. The star is the prism; Helios is what goes into it.

His virtue is Humility and his vice is Pride, the oldest pair: the one who stands at the center of everything is the one most tempted to believe he deserves to. The star that gives all light must not mistake itself for the light's owner — the Ruler's lesson too, for this is the royal body. And his cure crosses no axis: Humility cures Pride — the center heals itself.

That is also why he rules no single hour. Noon's Joy is Helios overhead, evening's Passion is Helios leaving, the pre-dawn Calm is the world holding its breath for him: every mood on the wheel is the same god at a different angle.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the center of the dial, white-gold, the source from which all six moods radiate. Sunday does not sit among the days — the days sit around Sunday.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette nothing about him changes — still the center, still white-gold. Every arm around him translates from pigment to beam, but white is the sum in both worlds; the source is the one point where the two readings agree.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-armed star Helios steps out of the center onto the 12h arm in the yellow of noon's Joy. The dawn and dusk arms now frame the day, and the sun consents to be one hand among eight — trading the center's white for the hue the day itself wears at its height.
variants.octa_light: The 12h arm again, but green — noon's Joy read through the prism. It looks strange on a sun until you recall that green is the exact middle of the visible spectrum, the band where sunlight is strongest: the light reading paints him not as he looks but as he measures.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross his arm at 12h is Summer, colored yellow, and he shares it with Zeus. The cross arms point at the solstices and equinoxes: this is the midsummer arm, the year's longest light, where Sunday's source and Thursday's king hold the peak together.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Helios keeps the center, and the star around him thins to three arms — Faith's yellow, Love's red, Hope's blue, the painter's own primaries. The founding sentence holds at its simplest: three virtues instead of six moods, and all of them still his one white light taken apart.

moon.base:
Selene is the moon as a person — not a goddess of the moon but the light itself, driving her own chariot through the night while her brother Helios sleeps. The drawing seats her in a crown horned with a crescent, her dark gown sown with stars and a crescent clasping her belt; a winged horse rears beside the great sickle moon behind her, a chariot wheel waits at her side, and in her open palm she holds the full moon itself — cratered, golden, offered like something being weighed. The Greeks told how she loved the shepherd Endymion and had him kept in eternal sleep: the one myth where watching over someone's night is the whole story.

Her home is the arm at dial 4h, painted deep blue — Monday's arm, the mood of Calm, the hour before dawn. The lower half of the dial is the night, and 4h is its stillest chamber, when even the sleepless finally go quiet. Blue was the phlegmatic Moon's color in the old humors, and silver is her metal: as gold crowns the star's noon tip, silver crowns its midnight one — day metal above, night metal below.

Her virtue is Serenity and her vice is Fear, and they are the same night twice: moonlit, the world is the calmest thing there is; unlit, it is the oldest terror. That is why she is the Physician — medieval medicine timed its cures by her phases, and the healer's calm hand is exactly what is set against the patient's fear. Across the dial stands Ares: Courage cures Fear, and Serenity quells Wrath — the soldier and the physician bound to one axis.

Calm rules the hour before dawn because that is where calm is earned. Anyone can be serene at noon; the 4h watch is serenity in the dark, with no proof yet that the sun returns — the moon holding the light until it does.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the 4h arm in the painter's deep blue, Monday's Calm in the hour before dawn.
variants.hexa_light: The same 4h arm, but the blue turns elemental: in the light wheel blue is one of the three primaries of light itself, not a mixture. Calm read through the prism is bedrock, not blend — the night as one of the world's fundamental colors.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-armed star she slides to 3h in a deeper sea-blue, and the mood is named simply the deep night. One hour closer to midnight, one shade further from dawn: the physician's watch moves to the darkest ward.
variants.octa_light: At 3h in the light palette her arm turns pure blue — the deepest of the night beams, a primary of light itself. Calm at full strength, as anyone who has watched the sky at three in the morning knows.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross she holds the 6h arm — Spring, green — and shares it with Cronus. The arm points at the spring equinox, the dawn of the year: the dial's two quiet teachers, Serenity and Patience, keeping the year's morning together.
variants.trio: The Trinity leaves Selene on her 4h arm but renames the post: the blue now means Hope, she shares the arm with Hermes, and with the pre-dawn hour at its very tip it guards the morning, 0h to 8h. The goddess who kept Endymion's night now guards the promise that night ends — calm turned to face the sun.

mars.base:
Ares is war without the parade — the Greeks gave him a planet and a weekday while keeping him at arm's length; in the Iliad, Zeus calls him the most hateful of the gods. The drawing is honest about it: he strides shouting under a crested helmet, spear in one fist and a shield embossed with a roaring lion's face in the other, while the storm behind him literally rains spears; a vulture waits at his left and a war-dog at his right — his sacred animals, which are also the battlefield's scavengers — and at his feet lie a broken wheel and someone else's fallen, eagle-blazoned shield.

His home is the arm at dial 16h, painted orange — Tuesday's arm, the mood of Zeal, the working afternoon. Orange is fire domesticated: not the noon blaze and not the evening blood-red, but the ember that gets things done. 16h is the hour of the second wind — effort as its own mood, the soldier's hour of drill and march rather than glory.

His virtue is Courage and his vice is Wrath, and the drawing refuses to separate them: the lion on the shield and the shout on his face are one fire, which defends or destroys depending only on where it is pointed. His cure hangs straight across the dial in Selene's blue: Serenity quells Wrath — and in return, Courage cures Fear. The soldier and the physician share an axis because they share a battlefield.

Zeal rules the working afternoon because that is where courage actually lives. Battles are rare; the long unglamorous march toward them is daily — and the ember-orange of 16h is the hour when persistence, not fury, carries the fight.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the 16h arm in ember orange, Tuesday's Zeal of the working afternoon.
variants.hexa_light: The same 16h arm, but the prism has no pigment orange — the ember flares to full yellow, red and green light summed. Zeal read as light is louder than zeal read as paint: the working fire at battle brightness.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-armed star he advances to 15h in amber, the mid-afternoon. One hour closer to noon's peak: the soldier moved up to the earlier watch, his fire nearer the height of the day.
variants.octa_light: At 15h in the light palette the yellow evens out, steadier and less blinding — the mid-afternoon as discipline rather than blaze. Wrath banked into routine is exactly what an army calls order.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross he stands at 18h — Autumn, red — and shares the arm with Aphrodite. It points at the autumn equinox, the year's dusk; the lovers whom Hephaestus once caught in a single net are bound to a single arm again, war and love ruling the season of the falling leaf together.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Ares marches to 20h and stands on Love's red arm — beside Aphrodite, as always, as the net proved. Together they guard the evening from 16h to 24h. Love reads through the war god as its garrison: the same fire that rains spears in his storm stands sentry so that what the heart keeps can sleep.

mercury.base:
Hermes is the god of everything that moves between: messages, merchants, roads, thieves, and finally souls — the only Olympian who walks freely into the underworld and back. The drawing catches him in a rare state, seated; but the winged cap and winged sandals stay on, the caduceus with its two twined serpents stands upright in his hand, and spare serpent-staffs lean at the pedestal's base beneath an arc of small stars — a courier between errands, never a courier retired.

His home is the arm at the very bottom of the dial, painted purple — Wednesday's arm, the mood of Sorrow, midnight. Why give the quickest god the darkest post? Because Hermes is the psychopomp, the guide of souls: midnight is the day's grave and its mystery, and someone must know the way through it. Purple is the mourning color, and the bottom tip he stands on carries silver, the night's metal.

His virtue is Wisdom and his vice is Greed — the merchant's pair, for the same cleverness that understands the world can begin merely counting it. His cure stands straight overhead: Zeus at noon, whose Generosity cures Greed — while Hermes' Wisdom, in return, curbs Excess. The dial's whole vertical axis is a conversation between the open hand and the counting one.

And Sorrow suits the hour of thought. Midnight is when the mind is most itself — accounts closed, roads empty, nothing left to trade but understanding; the god of exchange keeps the one watch where wisdom is the only currency.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the midnight arm at the dial's bottom, mourning purple, Wednesday's Sorrow.
variants.hexa_light: The same midnight post, but the purple becomes magenta — the prism's impossible color, found in no rainbow, existing only where the eye joins the two far ends of the spectrum. No hue suits the boundary-crosser better: a color that is itself a crossing.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-armed star he moves off midnight to 21h in deep red, the late evening. The guide takes the road earlier: still abroad while others turn in — the day's last messenger rather than its gravekeeper.
variants.octa_light: At 21h in the light palette the red is pure and full — the late evening as an unmixed beam. The day's final errand is run at signal-lamp brightness, urgency in the color of the heart rather than of mourning.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross he keeps the bottom of the dial — Winter, blue — and he shares the arm with no one. It points at the winter solstice, the year's own midnight: the guide of souls stands alone at that door, as he always has.
variants.trio: The Trinity calls Hermes up from midnight to 4h, onto Hope's blue arm beside Selene, guarding the morning from 0h to 8h. The psychopomp is handed the opposite door: not the guide down into the dark but the herald of what comes — hope being wisdom's forward road, and roads were always his.

jupiter.base:
Zeus is the storm made into law — the sky-father who ended the age of the Titans and then had to learn to govern the unruly family he freed. The drawing gives him the full regalia of that office: crowned and enthroned, a golden thunderbolt gripped in one fist and an eagle-topped scepter in the other, eagles carved into the throne's armrests and a live one circling above, while behind the throne rises an oak heavy with acorns — the tree of his oracle at Dodona, where the god spoke through rustling leaves.

His home is the top of the dial, the 12h arm, painted yellow — Thursday's arm, the mood of Joy at noon, the day's peak. The language remembers it: jovial literally means 'of Jupiter.' The king takes the crown position, where the real sun pours its gold straight down onto his arm; and Thursday is Guruvar in the Vedic week, the day of Brihaspati, teacher of the gods — the king's arm is also the teacher's.

His virtue is Generosity and his vice is Excess — the royal table that feeds everyone and then cannot stop. It is one hand that never closes, whether giving or reaching for a second portion. His cure stands at the opposite tip of the dial: Hermes' midnight Wisdom curbs Excess, while Zeus' Generosity, poured straight down the same axis, cures Greed.

Joy rules noon because noon hides nothing: everything stands in full light, shadows at their shortest, the world at its most given. Generosity is exactly that — the noon of the heart — and its excess is what happens when the feast refuses to admit the afternoon exists.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the noon arm at the dial's top, royal yellow, Thursday's Joy at the day's peak.
variants.hexa_light: The same crown position, but the prism turns his yellow to green — light's own primary at the top of the wheel, and the band the human eye sees most keenly. In the light reading the king wears the color of maximum sight: nothing at noon escapes him.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-armed star Zeus yields the zenith to Helios and moves to 9h in green, the mid-morning. It is a graceful abdication — the king steps aside for the source itself — and lands on the hour of held court: mid-morning, when a ruler actually rules.
variants.octa_light: At 9h in the light palette his arm turns cyan — the mid-morning sky itself. The thunder-god in plain daylight blue-green: the storm at rest, the sky simply being generous with the day.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross he returns to the top: 12h, Summer, yellow, sharing the arm with Helios. It points at the summer solstice — the year's longest light — where Thursday's king and Sunday's source hold the peak together, as throne and sun have always shared a summit.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Zeus keeps the top: the 12h arm, yellow — but the yellow is Faith's now, and he shares it with Cronus, the father he overthrew. Together they guard the day's height from 8h to 16h. Faith reads strangely and rightly through the king: the keeper of oaths stands on the arm of what every oath is made of.

venus.base:
Aphrodite was born of sea-foam, armed with nothing, and conquered everyone anyway. The drawing seats her in calm possession of her two emblems: the scallop shell of her birth in one hand, and in the other, raised like a verdict, the golden apple inscribed 'to the fairest' — the prize that flattered one goddess, enraged two, and burned Troy. Dove-topped staffs wreathed in roses stand around her throne, and small hearts float among the stars: the picture is all tenderness, holding the war quietly in one raised hand.

Her home is the arm at dial 20h, painted deep red — Friday's arm, the mood of Passion, the evening. Venus is the evening star, the first light to appear at dusk, and 20h is the hour the working day releases people to one another. Red is doubly hers: the sunset's blood and the heart's color, one pigment for the sky and for the pulse.

Her virtue is Love and her vice is Jealousy — not love's opposite but its shadow, the fear of losing what is loved; the apple in her hand is proof of how quickly 'fairest' becomes a wound. Her cure stands across the dial in Cronus' morning green: Patience heals Jealousy — and her Love, in return, dissolves Envy. She is the Artist, for the old woodcuts drew Venus' children as musicians and lovers: art is love given a form that cannot be taken away.

Passion rules the evening because passion is what the day was for. Work fills the light; what the work was in service of appears when the light goes. And Love alone also belongs to the theological trio of Faith, Hope and Love — the dial's human warmth, among people, on Friday evening.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the 20h arm in deep red, Friday's Passion of the evening.
variants.hexa_light: The same evening arm, but the red goes pure — light's first primary at full strength. In the prism world passion is not a pigment mixed on a palette but an elemental beam: the one color that needs nothing added.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-armed star she moves to 18h and takes the dusk arm itself, red-orange, and the mood changes name: from Passion to Longing, the emotion that looks back. The evening star at the exact hour of the sun's leaving — desire and farewell in one color.
variants.octa_light: At 18h in the light palette the dusk burns a brighter orange — the sunset sky's own hue read as light. Longing at full luminance: what has passed, still glowing.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross she stands at 18h — Autumn, red — sharing the arm with Ares. It points at the autumn equinox, and the pairing is the oldest gossip on Olympus: the lovers Hephaestus once trapped in a single net now share a single arm, love and war ruling the season of ripeness and fall.
variants.trio: Aphrodite does not move: the Trinity's Love arm is her own 20h in her own red, for Love alone belongs to both sets — arm virtue and theological virtue at once. Ares joins her, and the lovers guard the evening from 16h to 24h: the third of the day when work is done and only what one loves remains.

saturn.base:
Cronus is the hardest of the seven images, and it should be. The crowned titan glowers from a stone throne with a great scythe across his body; in his left hand he cradles a clock face with Roman numerals, gearwork showing behind it, and a toothed cog-ring circles his crown like a mechanical halo — the drawing deliberately fuses Cronus the titan with Chronos, time itself, a pun the Greeks were already making. In the upper corner floats a small winged child stained with blood: the memory of the children he swallowed. Behind him stands a bare, gnarled tree; near his feet, a young golden one — the old growth and the new.

His home is the arm at dial 8h, painted green — Saturday's arm, the mood of Renewal, the morning. The pairing looks like a paradox — the devourer on the arm of new growth — until you see the Farmer in him: Cronus carries the harvest sickle, and in Hebrew the planet is Shabtai, named for the Sabbath itself. The slowest planet keeps the day of rest, and green is dew, growth, and the week beginning again.

His virtue is Patience and his vice is Envy — the farmer's pair: he sows and waits, and eyes the neighbor's field. Envy is the bitter green of another's harvest, renewal's own color turned sour; Cronus swallowed his children because he could not endure being succeeded — envy of the future itself. His cure stands across the dial in Aphrodite's evening red: Love dissolves Envy — and his Patience, in return, heals Jealousy.

Renewal rules the morning because renewal is patience paid out. The scythe cuts so the field can grow again; the night ends because it was waited through. On the day of rest the green arm says quietly: what you did not seize will grow back — and that is the whole cure.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the 8h arm in harvest green, Saturday's Renewal of the morning.
variants.hexa_light: The same morning arm, but the green becomes cyan — the color of the early sky rather than the leaf. In the light reading renewal belongs to the air, not the field: the morning before anything has been planted at all.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-armed star the old titan takes the dawn arm itself: 6h, cyan, and the mood is Hope — the emotion that looks forward. It is the dial's gentlest irony: the keeper of time is given the hour when time begins again; patience's reward is dawn.
variants.octa_light: At 6h in the light palette the dawn deepens to a strong sky-blue. Hope read as light, on the arm of the god who tried to swallow the future — and now stands guard over its arrival each morning.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross he keeps 6h — Spring, green — and shares the arm with Selene. It points at the spring equinox, the year's own dawn: Selene's Serenity and his Patience, the dial's two quiet virtues, holding the childhood of the year together.
variants.trio: The Trinity lifts Cronus to the crown: the 12h Faith arm, yellow, shared with Zeus — the devourer and the child who escaped him on one arm, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. Faith is the titan's hardest lesson read forward: to trust the future he once swallowed, and wait for it with open hands.

sun.base:
In the North the sun is a woman. Sol stands in full scale armor with golden hair streaming, and she does not wear the sun — she holds it up in one raised hand, a blazing disc lifted like a lamp for the world, while behind her a two-horse chariot races above the waves: Arvakr and Alsvidr, Early-Waker and All-Swift, who pull the sun across the sky with the wolf Skoll forever at their heels. Her light is not a possession; it is a duty performed at a run.

On the dial she sits nowhere and everywhere: the center, white-gold, Sunday. The Sun is not one color among six — white light is the sum of all colors, so the six arms are her light decomposed, the star is the prism, and she is the source. The top of the dial is solar noon and carries her metal, gold, as the day's crown; but Sol herself belongs to no single hour, because every hour is lit by her.

Her virtue is Humility and her vice is Pride — the oldest pair, and the only axis that folds onto itself: the center heals the center, Humility being the one cure for Pride. Sol is the ruler's arm, and the ruler's lesson is hers exactly: the one who gives all light must not mistake herself for the light's owner. The myth builds the lesson into her body — pursued by the wolf, she cannot stop to be admired; the sun that lingered to receive worship would be devoured.

So her mood is not one of the six but their spring: Joy at noon, Sorrow at midnight, Calm, Zeal, Passion and Renewal are all one white ray, taken apart. Whoever reads any arm of the dial is reading a piece of her.

variants.hexa_paint: The home reading: the center of the dial, white-gold, the source from which all six arms decompose. Sunday does not ride the wheel of hours — it is the hub the wheel turns on.
variants.hexa_light: The prism reading repaints every arm, but not her: white light is white in both worlds, the one color that pigment and beam agree on. Sol alone stands unchanged at the center while the week shifts around her.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-pointed star she steps out of the center onto the top arm at dial 12h, yellow (#F8E600), into Joy — the noon peak. The source shows itself at its own hour: for once the sun stands IN the day instead of behind it, wearing the day's own gold.
variants.octa_light: The octa prism keeps her on the 12h arm but clothes her in green (#00DC00), the middle band of the spectrum — noon's Joy read as light. The white goddess wears the hour's color instead of her own.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons palette: her arm at dial 12h points at the summer solstice, colored summer yellow, and she shares the arm with Thor. Sun goddess and thunder god hold the season of longest light together — the sky's two loudest voices on one arm.
variants.trio: The Trinity is the theological palette: Sol returns to the hub with three arms only — Faith's yellow, Love's red, Hope's blue — all decomposed from her white-gold center. Faith radiates from her outward, and each arm fences one third of her daily run: the wolf-chased goddess remains the still point of the virtues.

moon.base:
Mani, Sol's brother, steers the moon. The relief shows him as an older god with long silver hair and a braided beard, bronze scale armor under a night-blue cloak spangled with tiny stars; his raised right hand holds a silver crescent, his left cradles a silver orb marked with its own crescent — the moon shown twice, as phase and as whole. Behind him his chariot rides above the waves, for he too is hunted, by the wolf Hati, and he too never stops: the old texts say he governs the moon's waxing and waning, and men counted their nights by him before they counted days.

His home is the 04h arm in deep blue (#002FFF), Monday's arm, the hour before dawn, and the mood is Calm. Blue is the still, moonlit hour — the phlegmatic Moon of the old physicians, whose whole art ran on his phases; the sickbed watch is kept at exactly this hour of the night, and it is kept by his profession, the Physician.

Blue carries both his faces: Serenity, the stillness of a moonlit world, and Fear, the same darkness unlit. Mani earns the virtue honestly — serenity is not the absence of the wolf but a steady hand despite it, the healer's calm against the patient's dread. Across the dial at 16h stands Tyr: Tyr's Courage cures Mani's Fear, and Mani's Serenity quells Tyr's Wrath — the soldier and the physician healing each other along one axis.

And that is why his mood rules the pre-dawn: it is the deepest quiet of the night, when fear is nearest and calm is worth most. The dial gives the last hour of darkness to the god who has driven through it every night since the beginning.

variants.hexa_paint: The home position: the 04h arm, deep pigment blue (#002FFF), the Calm of the hour before dawn — Monday resting one arm below the sunrise line.
variants.hexa_light: Same arm, same hour, but the pigment gives way to the prism's pure blue (#0040FF) — Calm read as light. Of all the arms Mani's changes least between the two worlds; the moon is the moon in any physics.
variants.octa_paint: The eight-pointed star gives 06h to the dawn itself, so Mani retreats to the 3h arm in sea-deep blue (#005ADC) — the deep night. He moves an hour further from sunrise, deeper into his own kingdom.
variants.octa_light: On the octa prism the 3h arm turns pure blue (#0000FF): the deep night as a primary beam, calm at its darkest and most certain — the moonlit world with nothing between it and the dark.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons palette: his arm at dial 6h points at the spring equinox, colored spring green, shared with Loki. The moon-steerer stands on the sunrise arm in the season of first growth — the counter of nights lending his silver to the year's green morning.
variants.trio: The Trinity keeps Mani at his 04h post but changes the watchword: the blue arm (#002FFF) now stands for Hope, shared with Odin, and it guards the morning from 0h to 8h, the pre-dawn calm at its tip. A right duty for the hunted moon-steerer: he has proven every single night that dawn arrives.

mars.base:
Tyr raises his right arm like a banner, and the arm ends at the wrist: a bandage-wrapped, blood-streaked stump, held high and unhidden, with a snarling wolf's head at his shoulder and a bloodied sword gripped point-down in his remaining hand, runes arcing through the field around him. The stump is the whole story — when the gods bound Fenrir with a ribbon they swore was harmless, only Tyr would lay his hand in the wolf's mouth as pledge, knowing the pledge was false and the hand was forfeit. Northern courage is not fearlessness; it is paying an honest price with open eyes.

His home is the 16h arm in orange (#DC9600) — Tuesday, which is literally Tyr's day, Tysdag. Orange is Mars' ember, the fire of the working afternoon, and its mood is Zeal: not the flash of battle but the discipline that keeps pushing when the day is long. It is the Soldier's arm, and Tyr is the soldier's god twice over — god of war and god of justice, the sword and the oath in one figure.

The ember has two faces, and the image shows both in one blade: Courage is the virtue, Wrath the vice — one fire that defends or destroys, the bloodied sword equally able to be either. His cure stands straight across the dial at 04h: Mani's Serenity quells Tyr's Wrath, and Tyr's Courage cures Mani's Fear. The one-handed god who did not flinch is the exact medicine for the hunted moon's dread.

Zeal rules the working afternoon because that is when courage is actually spent — not at dawn when strength is fresh, but at 16h, past the peak, when the day demands what a soldier has: the will to hold the line after comfort is gone.

variants.hexa_paint: The home position: the 16h arm, ember orange (#DC9600), the Zeal of the working afternoon — Tuesday's fire banked into steady heat.
variants.hexa_light: Same 16h arm, but the prism burns the ember to bright yellow (#FFFF00): zeal read as raw light instead of glowing coal — the afternoon at its most blinding.
variants.octa_paint: With dusk claiming the 18h arm, Tyr shifts to 15h in amber (#ECB800) — the mid-afternoon. He moves an hour toward noon, closer to the day's peak, the soldier called up earlier in the day.
variants.octa_light: On the octa prism the 15h arm holds a hard field-yellow (#DCDC00): the mid-afternoon in light hues, zeal turned metallic — the color of work without shade.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons palette: his arm at dial 18h points at the autumn equinox, colored autumn red, and he shares it with Freya. War and love on one sunset arm — the old Mars-and-Venus pairing in northern dress, the sword-hand and the sword-maiden holding the year's red season together.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Tyr shifts to 20h and stands under Love's deep red (#B60000), sharing the arm with Freya — sword-hand and sword-maiden guarding the evening from 16h to 24h. Love reads through Tyr as the pledge: he gave his hand so the others would be safe, which is love spelled in the north's own alphabet.

mercury.base:
Odin here is the wanderer-sage with the ledger of prices paid: an eyepatch over the eye he traded into Mimir's well for one drink of wisdom, the spear Gungnir in his fist with runes glowing faintly along the shaft — runes he won by hanging nine nights on the world-tree, a sacrifice of himself to himself — and his two ravens, Huginn and Muninn, Thought and Memory, one perched on the spear-arm and one at his shoulder, with a great bare tree rising behind him. Everything he has, he bought; nothing was given.

His home is the bottom of the dial, the 0h arm at midnight, in mourning purple (#542E85) — Wednesday, Odin's own day, Wodnesdag. Mercury's arm is the mind itself, and midnight is where minds like his do their work: the god of the hanged, the whisperer with the dead, the seeker who goes down into the dark to bring knowledge back up. The arm's profession is the Merchant, and it fits him strangely well — Odin never receives, he only trades: an eye for wisdom, nine nights for the runes, everything at a price.

Purple carries his pair: Wisdom is the virtue, Greed the vice — because a hunger for knowing, pursued without end, takes greed's exact shape even when the currency is insight rather than gold. The cure hangs straight overhead: his son Thor at noon. Generosity cures Greed, and Wisdom curbs Excess — father and son healing each other across the full height of the dial, midnight to noon.

And Sorrow rules his hour because Odin's wisdom has a specific weight: he knows how it ends. Ragnarok is not a rumor to him but a certainty carried daily; the one-eyed god sees further than anyone and mourns most of what he sees. Midnight, the day's grave and its mystery, is the only hour deep enough for him.

variants.hexa_paint: The home position: the 0h arm at the very bottom of the dial, mourning purple (#542E85), the Sorrow of midnight — Wednesday at the day's nadir.
variants.hexa_light: Same midnight arm, but the prism electrifies the purple into magenta (#BD00BD): sorrow with a neon edge, the midnight mystery read as strange light rather than mourning cloth.
variants.octa_paint: The octa wheel moves him to the 21h arm in deep red (#DC0000) — the late evening. Odin steps back from the grave-hour toward the living end of night, the hour of last fires and long tales.
variants.octa_light: On the octa prism the 21h arm burns pure red (#FF0000): the late evening at full saturation — the wanderer seen against the last light instead of the dark.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons palette: he keeps the 0h arm, pointed at the winter solstice and colored winter blue — and alone. Every other body shares its seasonal arm; Odin has no partner. The god of the longest night holds the year's longest night by himself, which is exactly how he travels.
variants.trio: The Trinity raises Odin from midnight to the 4h arm of Hope, blue (#002FFF), shared with Mani — for once the god of the longest night is not alone. Hope sits strangely on the one who knows how it all ends, and that is the point: he gathers wisdom against Ragnarok anyway. Hope is what Odin does, not what he feels.

jupiter.base:
Thor is shown at full swing: winged helmet, braided beard, both hands locked on the short haft of Mjolnir with the rune-carved hammer swept across his body, knees bent in a wide braced stance, the girdle of might at his waist and the cape flaring behind him. It is not a portrait of majesty but of labor — thunder is not something Thor has, it is something he does, and the whole relief is caught mid-blow.

His home is the top of the dial: the 12h arm in noon yellow (#F8E600), Thursday, Thor's own day. Jupiter is the jovial planet — 'jovial' literally means 'of Jupiter' — and Thor is the gods' laugh made flesh, the protector everyone toasts. The arm's profession is the Priest and Teacher, and the hammer earns it: Mjolnir hallowed weddings, births and funerals — the thunder god was the blessing god, the one instrument that consecrated and defended alike.

Yellow holds his two faces: Generosity is the virtue, Excess the vice — the open hand and the overflowing table, and no table in myth overflows like Thor's, who at Thrym's feast ate an ox and eight salmon and drank three casks of mead while disguised as a bride. He guards Midgard and asks nothing for it; he also never stops at enough. The curb hangs straight below at midnight: his father Odin, whose Wisdom curbs Thor's Excess, while Thor's Generosity cures Odin's Greed — one axis, father and son, noon and midnight.

Joy rules his hour because noon is the day's peak and the peak belongs to its defender: the hammer rests at the top of the dial where the sun stands highest, and the mood of that height is exactly what Thursday's god brings into a room — appetite, laughter, and the certainty that the roof will hold.

variants.hexa_paint: The home position: the 12h arm crowning the dial, noon yellow (#F8E600), the Joy of the day's peak — Thursday at the top of the world.
variants.hexa_light: Same crowning arm, but the prism turns noon green (#00DC00) — the middle band of the spectrum for the middle of the day. Joy read as light: the peak hour in the color of full leaf.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-pointed star Sol herself takes the noon arm, so Thor steps to 9h in green (#00DC00) — the mid-morning. The protector moves to the working hours before the peak, hammer up while the day is still being built.
variants.octa_light: The octa prism gives his 9h arm cyan (#00FFFF): the mid-morning as clear sky after a storm — the thunder god's own aftermath color.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons palette: his arm at dial 12h points at the summer solstice, colored summer yellow, shared with Sol. Thunder and sun together on the summer arm — the season of the highest light and the heaviest storms, both of them his.
variants.trio: On the Trinity Thor keeps the crown at 12h in Faith's yellow (#F8E600), sharing the arm with Loki — his old road-companion made his arm-mate — as the two guard the day's height from 8h to 16h. Faith reads plainest through Thor of all the gods: Midgard trusts that the hammer will come, and it always has.

venus.base:
Freya stands double-armed: a raised sword in one hand and a single golden falcon feather in the other — her feather-cloak, which carries its wearer between worlds — with a winged diadem on her brow, the amber-stoned Brisingamen at her throat, and at her side a boar and the prow of a longship. The image tells the truth the Roman Venus hides: the northern love goddess is also a war goddess, chooser of the slain who takes half the battle-dead before Odin picks. Love and the sword in the same two hands, neither one lowered.

Her home is the 20h arm in deep red (#B60000) — Friday, Freya's day, the evening hour whose mood is Passion. Red is the sunset's blood, and Venus is the evening star that rises exactly then; hers is the Artist's arm, the planet whose 'children' in the old woodcuts were musicians and lovers — the hour when the day's work ends and the day's wanting begins.

Red carries her pair: Love is the virtue, Jealousy the vice — the fear of losing what is loved, and Freya's myths are crowded with coveting: the Brisingamen bargained for and stolen, giants naming her as the price of every ransom. Her cure stands straight across at 08h, and the dial's wit is sharp here: it is Loki, the very thief who stole her necklace. His arm's Patience heals her Jealousy, and her Love dissolves his Envy — owner and thief prescribed to each other across the whole dial.

Passion rules the evening because that is when it truly lives — not at noon among the day's duties but at 20h, when the light turns red and people turn to what, and whom, they actually want.

variants.hexa_paint: The home position: the 20h arm, deep pigment red (#B60000), the Passion of the evening — Friday glowing where the evening star rises.
variants.hexa_light: Same evening arm, but the prism saturates the red to full light (#FF0000): passion as a beam instead of an ember — the evening at maximum intensity.
variants.octa_paint: The eight-pointed star moves her to the 18h dusk arm in sunset orange (#DC9600), and the mood becomes Longing — her own myth exactly: Freya wandering the world weeping tears of red gold for her lost husband Odr. Passion looks forward; longing looks back down the road he took.
variants.octa_light: On the octa prism the dusk arm burns bright orange (#FF8C00): Longing in the sky's own sunset color, the burning west read as light.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons palette: her arm at dial 18h points at the autumn equinox, colored autumn red, shared with Tyr. The sword-maiden and the one-handed soldier hold the red season together — love and war on one arm, as the North always knew they were.
variants.trio: Freya stands still: the Trinity's Love arm is her own 20h in her own red (#B60000), for Love alone belongs to both sets. Tyr joins her, and together they guard the evening from 16h to 24h — the chooser of the slain and the one-handed pledge-giver guarding the dark third, love with a sword in each hand.

saturn.base:
Loki grins out of the relief — the only smiling god on the dial. Red-gold braids frame the smirk; one hand grips a spear whose head glows poison-green above green-lit runes, the other lifts a golden woven net; a serpent coils over a small fire at his feet and a wolf snarls at the lower edge. Every object is either his invention or his sentence: the net is the fisherman's tool the gods rebuilt from his own ashes to catch him as a salmon, the serpent is the one that drips venom on his bound face, the wolf is Fenrir, his son. Cleverness and punishment share one frame.

His home is the 08h arm in green (#007E00) — Saturday. Old Norse gave that day no god at all, only laugardagr, washing day; the owner's canon seats Loki in Cronus' empty chair, the bound trickster as the northern mirror of the bound Titan. The arm's hour is morning, its mood Renewal, its profession the Farmer — and renewal is Loki's paradox: nothing in the myths changes, breaks open, or grows back without him. Even his fire clears the field.

Green carries his pair with brutal precision: Envy is the vice — his envy of Baldr, the god everyone loved, drove the dial's darkest deed — and Patience is the virtue, taught to him the hard way: bound to the rock beneath the dripping serpent until the world's end, the trickster's sentence is nothing but waiting. His cure stands across at 20h, and it is Freya, whose necklace he stole: her Love dissolves his Envy, and his arm's Patience heals her Jealousy — thief and owner healing each other along one axis.

So Renewal rules his morning honestly: the farmer sows, waits, and eyes the neighbor's field; the week rests and restarts on Saturday; and after Ragnarok — the fire Loki himself pilots — the world comes back green. The morning arm belongs to the one who proves, at the highest price on the dial, that endings are how the story grows.

variants.hexa_paint: The home position: the 08h arm, morning green (#007E00), the Renewal of the young day — Saturday, the week's rest and restart.
variants.hexa_light: Same morning arm, but the prism turns the green to cyan (#00DDDD) — renewal read as water-light, fitting for the god caught as a salmon on the day the North called washing day.
variants.octa_paint: The eight-pointed star moves him to the 6h dawn arm in cyan (#00DCDC), and the mood becomes Hope — a strange grace for the bound one, yet earned: even Ragnarok precedes a world reborn, and the dawn belongs to whoever waited out the night.
variants.octa_light: On the octa prism the dawn arm deepens to sky blue (#0080FF): Hope in the color of the pre-sunrise sky — the trickster holding the hour that looks only forward.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons palette: his arm at dial 6h points at the spring equinox, colored spring green, shared with Mani. The bound god and the hunted moon hold the sunrise arm together — spring, the season that forgives winter everything, on the arm of the one who caused it.
variants.trio: The Trinity gives Loki the strangest promotion on the dial: the 12h Faith arm, yellow (#F8E600), beside Thor, guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. Faith on the deceiver reads as sentence and prophecy at once — the gods' broken trust bound him, yet after his own fire the world comes back green. Even Loki serves the promise.

sun.name: Christianity
sun.base:
Christianity is the faith that God entered history from below: the crucified and risen Christ, and a love that answers death with resurrection.

The owner's medallion sets a glowing white cross on black stone, the Chi-Rho monogram at its crossing and a crown of thorns lying at its foot; the silver rim weaves grapevines with tiny ichthys fish - the vine and the fishermen, the oldest signs of the church.

It keeps Sunday because Sunday is the Lord's Day, the morning of the empty tomb; in 321 Constantine fused the day of the Sun with the day of rest, and the fusion held. So it sits on no arm but at the white-gold center - the source whose light the six arms decompose.

The center's pair is Humility and Pride, and no teaching states that axis harder: the King who washes feet, the God who empties himself. Humility cures Pride - the center heals itself.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexa pointer's paint wheel - yellow, red and blue mixed into orange, green and purple - Christianity holds the white-gold center. Pigment cannot mix white; it must be given, not blended. The painter's world thus preaches its sermon for it: the source is not one of the six, it is what the six are made from.
variants.hexa_light: In the light world the truth inverts and agrees: red, green and blue beams sum to white, so the hexa light pointer makes the center literal - six emissions converging into one radiance. For a faith whose founder said 'I am the light of the world', no palette could be more its own.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-pointed octa star even the center finally takes an arm: Christianity steps onto the 12h point in noon's yellow, the Lord's Day standing at the top of the wheel. The white-gold hub is not abandoned — every mixed color still looks inward to the unmixed source — but for once the faith of the Sun's day also stands IN the week it begins.
variants.octa_light: The octa light pointer rings the hub with eight glowing arms, and Christianity remains the still point they pour from. More beams only make the white at the center whiter - an image of grace the faith would recognize: the gifts differ, the giver is one.
variants.cross: The cross pointer marks the four seasons, and Christianity again takes the center where the arms meet - fitting, since the cross is its own sign, the same shape that glows on the owner's medallion. The seasons wheel around it like the liturgical year around Easter: every arm points back to the crossing.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Sun keeps the center, and its theme is Faith - the vertical line toward God. Faith's yellow, Hope's blue and Love's red all decompose from its white light, which is the trio's whole theology in one gesture: for Christianity, faith, hope and love are one light seen three ways.
