Venus opens over the western horizon just after sunset — precisely the sky Hathor rules as Lady of the West — so goddess and planet literally share a station: the most beautiful light in the heavens standing over the gate of the dead. On the hexa paint dial her arm is 20h, deep red, the mood of Passion — the hour when work ends and wanting begins, when the sunset bleeds and the music starts at Dendera.

Love is her virtue and Jealousy her vice — love's shadow, not its opposite. Egypt told that shadow plainly: as the Eye of Ra she withdrew from the land in bitterness and had to be coaxed home with music and dance, and once, unleashed, only beer dyed red as blood could still her — love turned resentful until joy calls it back. The cure stands straight across the dial at 8h, on the green morning arm this theme gives to Osiris: Patience heals Jealousy, and Hathor's Love flows back across the axis to dissolve Envy. It is the dial's most Egyptian axis — the Lady of the West delivers the dead to the lord of rebirth, and love and patience have passed between those two since the first sunset.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm: 20h, blood red, Passion — the Lady of the West on the arm of the evening she rules, the sistrum shaking as the sun goes down into her country.
variants.hexa_light: The light palette keeps Hathor at 20h and turns her red to the prism's full voltage — pure red, light's own primary. Passion undiluted: the goddess of the drunkenness festival poured the strongest red that light itself can mix.
variants.octa_paint: The octa wheel moves Hathor onto the 18h dusk arm itself, glowing orange — the mood of Longing, the emotion that looks back. No move on the wheel fits a god more exactly: the Lady of the West standing at the moment of sunset, receiving the day as it dies into her evening land.
variants.octa_light: At 18h in light colors Hathor burns sunset-orange, the sky's own dusk hue — Longing as the west going down in fire, the whole horizon glowing the exact complement of her turquoise medallion.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Hathor holds the 18h arm, red for Autumn, sharing it with Montu — love and war on one arm, as Egypt itself told it: the lady of joy and the Eye of Ra are one goddess in two moods, and Thebes worshipped her beside its falcon of battle. The autumn equinox is the year's dusk — the harvest of what was loved, carried west.
variants.trio: Hathor does not move at all: the Trinity's red Love arm is her own 20h, and Love is already her whole estate — the one virtue standing in both the seven pairs and the theological three. Montu joins her there, and together they guard the evening from 16h to 24h: music on one side of the door and a raised arm on the other, passion and courage keeping the dark third of the day.

saturn.base:
Before he was lord of the dead, Osiris was the king who taught Egypt to grow grain — the myth remembers him as the first civilizer, murdered and cut into pieces by his brother Set, who envied the throne. Isis gathered him, love reassembling what envy had scattered, and the risen god took the throne of the underworld instead: judge of hearts, first of the westerners, the one every Egyptian hoped to become at death — the funerary texts promote each soul into 'an Osiris'. His death and return were read into everything that cycles: the Nile flood, the buried seed, the grain that stands again.

The owner's medallion holds him in bronze against a field of glowing malachite green: mummiform, wrapped in banded linen, wearing the tall atef crown — the pale cone of Upper Egypt between two ribbed ostrich plumes — with the crook and flail crossed at his chest, the crook striped in lapis blue and gold, and an ankh knotted at his belt: the key of life worn by the lord of the dead. The rim answers him in kind, wheat sheaves braided with lotus and ankhs around the whole border — a harvest woven into metal.

He rules Saturday because Osiris is Saturn's patience made a god. Cronus carries the harvest sickle; Osiris owned the harvest first, and the flail on his chest is a threshing tool as much as a scepter. Saturn takes nearly thirty years to close one circle, and no figure in any pantheon out-waits Osiris — he waits out death itself and comes back green. On the day of rest, whose planet in Hebrew is Shabtai after the Sabbath, Egypt's god rests in the earth the way seed does: temporarily.

The arm gives him green at 8h, the morning, the mood of Renewal — and Egypt agreed millennia in advance, painting Osiris's skin green as new growth, exactly the field his medallion glows with. Patience is the arm's virtue: the seed's whole art is waiting in the dark. Envy is its vice, and Osiris knows it from the receiving end — Set's envy is the engine of the entire myth. The cure stands across the dial on the red evening arm: Venus' Love — Hathor's, in this theme — dissolves Envy, just as Isis's love regathered the dismembered king; and Saturn's Patience travels back to heal Venus' Jealousy — the waiting art and the loving art mending each other.

variants.hexa_paint: The home arm: 8h, morning green (#007E00), Renewal — Saturday, the week's rest and restart. Paint green is Osiris's own pigment: tomb painters gave him skin this color to mean rebirth, and the medallion's malachite field simply widens it to fill the arm.
variants.hexa_light: The light palette keeps him at 8h but turns the green to cyan (#00DDDD) — morning read as water-light rather than field. For Osiris that is the Nile's color: the flood that buried the fields each year and left them fertile was called his coming.
variants.octa_paint: The octa wheel moves him onto the 6h dawn arm itself, cyan (#00DCDC), the mood of Hope. No god carries that arm more literally: to hope, in Egypt, was to expect to rise as an Osiris — hope is patience turned to face forward, and the lord of rebirth earns the arm that waits for the sun.
variants.octa_light: At 6h in light colors he wears the sky blue (#0080FF) of the last minutes before sunrise — the hour Ra's barque leaves the underworld. Every night the sun passed through Osiris's kingdom and was renewed there; the Hope this arm holds is his nightly work, delivered at dawn.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Osiris holds the 6h arm, green for Spring, pointed at the vernal equinox and shared with the Moon — Khonsu in this theme. Sowing season is his festival in the most literal sense: Egyptians planted 'Osiris beds', seed grain in god-shaped molds, and watched the deity himself sprout.
variants.trio: The Trinity promotes him from the morning to the crown: the 12h Faith arm, yellow (#F8E600), shared with Jupiter — Amun here — guarding the day's height from 8h to 16h. Faith reads through Osiris as the oldest resurrection promise there is: every tomb in Egypt was built on trust that what goes into the earth comes back — the harvest creed spoken toward the sky.

sun.base:
Dažbog is the giving sun — his very name binds dati, to give, to bog, god: the god who gives. He is the son of Svarog the sky-smith, and he rides the daylight across the heavens in a chariot, dealing out warmth and grain and fortune to whoever stands beneath him. The Tale of Igor's Campaign calls the Slavs themselves Dažbog's grandchildren, vnutsi Dazhbozhi — a whole people descended from the sun, warmed by an ancestor who asks nothing and pours out everything.

The owner's medallion paints him as a radiant youth of silver skin crowned with a golden winged helm, an eagle spreading its wings above his brow, and behind his head a corona of long golden rays like a second sun. His cloak is beaten gold and stamped with sun-discs; in the crook of one arm he cradles a horn of plenty spilling silver coins and ripe wheat, and in the other hand he lifts a scepter capped with a blazing sun-star. Flames of amber and gold fill the field behind him, and the border prowls with wolves and glowing runes.

His day is Sunday — nedelja in the Slavic tongue, the 'no-work' day, the day the week rests in the light before it begins again. So Dažbog does not take an arm at all: he holds the CENTER of the dial, white-gold, the still hub from which the six colored arms radiate. He is the source the whole spectrum decomposes from — white light that only looks empty because it already contains every color the other gods will wear.

His axis is Humility against Pride. The sun gives all its light away and keeps none for itself; that endless open-handed giving is the deepest Humility, and its shadow is the Pride that would hoard the blaze and call the warmth its own. To sit at the center and be the source of everything, yet claim nothing — that is the discipline the giving god teaches his grandchildren.

variants.hexa_paint: In the paint palette the center is the one color no brush can reach: pigments mix by subtraction, and no blending of pigments ever yields white. So Dažbog's white-gold hub is the unmixable center — the source every colored arm is a piece of, yet none of them, mixed, can climb back up to. He can give the colors out; they can never give him back.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette the logic reverses: the six colored beams of the arms, summed at the hub, add up to exactly white. Here Dažbog's white-gold center is not a paint that cannot be mixed but a radiance that is the sum of all six — the giving sun proven by arithmetic, every arm's color returning to make the light whole again.
variants.octa_paint: The eight-pointed star has no center to sit in, so the sun steps out and takes the topmost 12h arm, burning in noon yellow at the very peak of the dial. Dažbog leaves the hub to stand at high noon — the giving god no longer the hidden source but the visible crown of the day, gold at the top of the sky where the light is strongest.
variants.octa_light: In the octa light palette eight beams radiate from the hub and the Sun stands at the top as pure emission — noon yellow thrown upward, the point where the star gives its brightest light to the sky. Dažbog is not the still center here but the flaring apex, the giving made into a single beam.
variants.cross: The seasons cross keeps a true crossing — four arms meeting at one point — and Dažbog keeps that meeting-place, the hub where the year's four quarters intersect. The giving sun holds the still center around which spring, summer, autumn and winter wheel, the fixed white-gold heart of the turning year.
variants.trio: In the trio — Faith, Hope, Love — the sun keeps the center, and from it the yellow Faith arm rises straight up the 12h vertical (Faith at top, Hope at 4h, Love at 20h). Dažbog is the white-gold source, and Faith is the first thing that climbs out of him toward noon: the giving light made into the upward-pointing virtue.

moon.base:
Hors is the light of the night — the cold luminary that rules the sky when the sun is gone. His name may carry the old Iranian root for glow or sun-disc, and he moves as a wheel of pale fire across the dark. The Tale of Igor's Campaign remembers him in one unforgettable line: prince Vseslav the sorcerer, running as a wolf through the night, 'crossed the path of great Hors' before the cockcrow — a werewolf racing the moon's own track and cutting across it in the last dark before dawn.

The owner's medallion shows a youth of silver-blue skin crowned with a horned crescent that cradles a full moon between its tips, the disc glowing with soft light above his brow. His cloak is silvered and stamped with a running row of moon-phases, from new to full and back again; in his hand he lifts a staff capped with a crescent. Behind him is deep starry night, and the whole border is crowded with crescents, silver stars and runes, one full moon riding at the top.

His day is Monday — ponedeljak, the day 'after nedelja,' the first step out of the day of rest and still half in the dark. Hors takes the 4h arm, low on the left of the dial, in deep blue — the hour just before dawn, when the night's light is thinnest and the world is most still. His mood is Calm: the held breath of the last dark hour before the sun returns.

His axis is Serenity against Fear. The dark is where fear breeds — every shape a threat, every sound a wolf — and Hors answers it with the Serenity of a steady night-light that does not flinch. His cure lies across the dial in the mars arm: the Courage of Svetovid is what dissolves the Fear that the moonlit dark invents. Calm holds the night; courage ends it.

variants.hexa_paint: In the paint palette Hors holds the 4h arm in a deep blue (#002FFF) — the pigment of the pre-dawn hour, night thinned but not yet broken. It is the mixing-blue of the darkest part of the wheel, the color the world wears in the last cold hour before Dažbog climbs back over the rim.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette the same 4h arm burns as pure primary blue (#0040FF) — one of the three beams the white hub decomposes into. Hors is no longer a mixed pigment but a fundamental ray: the blue that light itself is built from, the night's own frequency standing clean in the prism wheel.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-pointed star Hors slides down to the 3h arm, deeper into the night, in a sea-deep blue (#005ADC). The octa pushes him further from dawn — a darker, colder hour than the hexa gave him, the moon-light sunk toward the bottom of the night where the blue goes almost black.
variants.octa_light: In the octa light palette his arm resolves to pure blue (#0000FF) — the flat, saturated primary with nothing mixed in. Hors at his most elemental: the night reduced to a single unbroken beam of blue, the cold light of the sky made into one clean color.
variants.cross: The seasons cross moves Hors to the 6h arm, colored spring green and aimed straight at the vernal equinox — and here he shares the arm with Morana, the saturn body. The night-light and the winter-death stand on the same spring arm: for the dawn to come and the year to turn, the long dark and the frost must both give way at once.
variants.trio: In the trio Hors keeps the 4h arm, but it is now the Hope arm, colored Hope blue (#002FFF) — and he shares it with Veles, the mercury body. The last dark hour becomes the hour of Hope: the moon-light that outlasts the night is the promise that the sun will rise, blue leaning toward the coming gold.

mars.base:
Svetovid was the great war-god of the western Slavs, worshipped at the temple of Arkona on the island of Rügen until the Danes threw it down in 1168. His idol had FOUR faces, one gazing to each of the four directions, so that nothing on any horizon escaped him. In his hand he held a horn of mead that the priests refilled every year and read as an omen: a full horn promised a fat harvest, a low one warned of famine. And in the temple he kept a sacred WHITE HORSE that only the priest could ride — before any campaign they led it over crossed spears, and the foot it stepped with first told the army whether the god blessed the war.

The owner's medallion sets aside the four faces and paints Svetovid as a single grim warrior in dark iron armor and a winged helm, a great sword driven point-down into the ground before him, both the blade and his gaze steady. At his side rears the sacred WHITE HORSE, bright against a field of black stone cracked with glowing red fire, as if the whole ground were a forge. The border is a ring of iron shields, crossed spears and ember-red runes — a war-god's shield-wall drawn around him.

His day is Tuesday — utorak, simply 'the second,' the second day of labor. Svetovid takes the 16h arm on the lower right of the dial, burning ember orange, and his mood is Zeal — the hard driving heat of the working afternoon, the hour when the day's fight is fully joined and the arms swing without pause.

His axis is Courage against Wrath. The war-god is Courage itself — the nerve to lead the white horse over the spears and march — but his shadow is Wrath, the battle-heat that stops being brave and starts being blind. His cure waits across the dial in the moon arm: the Serenity of Hors is what quells the Wrath, the cool night-calm laid over the ember of the fight.

variants.hexa_paint: In the paint palette Svetovid holds the 16h arm in ember orange — a mixed pigment, red warmed with yellow, the exact color of iron pulled glowing from the forge. It is the afternoon at its hottest, the war-heat given a hue you can only reach by blending, a fighting color mixed from blood and fire.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette orange has no seat — the prism wheel knows no orange among its beams — so Svetovid's arm resolves to pure yellow instead. The war-god, denied his ember, stands in the raw yellow of open daylight: the Zeal is the same, but the color is stripped back to a ray the prism actually contains.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-pointed star Svetovid shifts to the 15h arm in amber (#ECB800) — mid-afternoon, a shade cooler and more golden than the hexa's ember. The octa pulls him a little earlier in the day, the war-heat caught just past its peak, amber rather than fire.
variants.octa_light: In the octa light palette the amber hardens into a brassy, metallic yellow — a colder, harder light than the paint. Svetovid's afternoon rings like struck bronze here, the Zeal beaten thin and bright, the war-god's hour turned to brass.
variants.cross: The seasons cross moves Svetovid to the 18h dusk arm, colored red and aimed at the autumn equinox — and he shares it with Mokoš, the venus body. The war-god and the earth-mother stand together on the arm of harvest's end: autumn is the season armies march and the season the fields are cut, dusk-red for both the blade and the sickle.
variants.trio: In the trio Svetovid moves to the 20h Love arm, colored red, standing beside Mokoš and guarding the evening third of the dial from 16h to 24h. The war-god posted on the Love arm is the guardian's paradox — the sword that stands watch so the evening can be loved in peace, Courage set to keep the hours it does not spend fighting.

mercury.base:
Veles — Volos in the old treaties — is the horned god of everything that lives low and moves in the dark: cattle and wealth, trade and cunning, music and magic, and the underworld of the dead. He is the 'cattle-god,' skotiy bog, and when the Rus' princes swore peace with Byzantium they swore it 'by Perun and by Volos' — the thunderer for their weapons, the cattle-god for their goods. He is the serpent coiled at the roots of the world-tree while Perun holds its crown, and he is the patron of poets: the Tale of Igor's Campaign calls the great bard Boyan 'Veles's grandson.'

The owner's medallion seats him as a horned, long-bearded god on a throne of living tree-roots, wrapped in a shaggy beast-pelt. In one hand he holds a shepherd's crook; in the other he cradles a lyre, for he is the god of song as well as of herds. Cattle and sheep graze around his knees, and a great serpent coils through the roots at his feet — the snake at the bottom of the world. The whole field glows underworld teal-green, and the border writhes with knotted serpents and horned animal skulls.

His day is Wednesday — sreda, 'the middle,' the hinge of the week. Veles takes the very BOTTOM of the dial, the 0h/24h arm at midnight, in mourning purple — the day's grave, the deepest dark, the mystery under everything. His mood is Sorrow. And his position is the myth itself: Veles at the bottom and Perun at the crown of the star is the Perun–Veles axis made literal — the serpent at the roots, the thunderer at the top, the two poles of the vertical the whole dial hangs from.

His axis is Wisdom against Greed. The underworld-god is Wisdom — the deep, buried knowing of the dead, the cunning of trade, the poet's craft — but the same appetite that gathers cattle and coin curdles into Greed, the hoard kept for its own sake. His cure is across the dial: the Generosity of the jupiter arm, the open hand of the thunder-god's rain, is what dissolves the serpent's Greed. Wisdom knows what to keep; generosity knows what to give away.

variants.hexa_paint: In the paint palette Veles holds the 0h arm in mourning purple (#542E85) — a mixed, bruised color, red and blue ground together into the hue of grief and midnight. It is the pigment of the day's grave, the deepest, most blended dark on the wheel, the color of the underworld the cattle-god keeps.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette his purple resolves to magenta (#BD00BD) — the strange color the prism itself cannot make, that only appears where the spectrum's two ends are bent around to touch. Fitting for the god of the boundary between living and dead: Veles wears the one hue that lives outside the rainbow, at the bottom where the ends meet.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-pointed star Veles rises to the 21h arm in deep red (#DC0000) — late evening rather than dead midnight. The octa lifts him out of the grave and into the last red hours before the dark, the cattle-god at the close of the day's trade, blood-red where the hexa gave him purple.
variants.octa_light: In the octa light palette the arm burns pure red (#FF0000) — the flat primary, the loudest color in the wheel. Veles at late evening turned to raw signal-red: the underworld-god pulled up out of his mourning purple into the one color that refuses to be quiet.
variants.cross: The seasons cross keeps Veles on the 0h arm, aimed at the winter solstice and colored winter blue — and alone. Every other body shares its seasonal arm; the cattle-god shares none. The god of the longest night holds the year's longest night by himself, the serpent at the bottom of the year as well as of the dial, unpartnered in the deepest dark.
variants.trio: In the trio Veles climbs to the 4h Hope arm, colored Hope blue (#002FFF), and shares it with Hors, the moon body. The underworld-god rises out of the grave to stand on the arm of Hope beside the night-light — the buried Wisdom leaning toward dawn, the serpent lifting its head at the promise of morning.

jupiter.base:
Perun is the thunder-god, the axe-bearer, the chief of the old Slavic sky. His tree is the oak, his weapon the axe and the hurled thunderstone, and his sign is the six-petalled thunder-mark — the gromovnik rosette that Slavs carved into their roof-beams to turn the lightning away, a six-armed star the clock's own hexagram pointer quietly echoes. His great myth is the storm itself: the serpent Veles steals the cattle and dams up the waters, and Perun hunts him across the sky and strikes him with the bolt — and the RAIN that bursts loose is the sky's generosity, the withheld water finally given back to the earth.

The owner's medallion shows him as a broad, bearded warrior in ornate armor and a winged helm, hurling a bolt of brilliant blue lightning from his raised right hand while a great battle-axe hangs ready in his left. Storm-clouds boil around him, mountains stand below, and forked lightning splits the sky. The border is woven from oak leaves and acorns — his sacred tree — set with lightning-bolts and runes, and a bull's head glowers from his belt.

His day is Thursday — četvrtak, 'the fourth' — and on the dial he takes the TOP, the 12h arm, the crown of the star, in noon yellow. His mood is Joy: the high, ringing gladness of the sky at its brightest, the thunder-god at the zenith. That the six-armed hexagram crowns him at the top is no accident — the pointer is his own thunder-mark, and he sits at the head of it.

His axis is Generosity against Excess. The rain he releases is pure Generosity — the sky opening its hand and pouring the hoarded water down onto the fields — but the same storm can drown what it means to feed, and that is Excess, the flood that gives too hard. His cure is across the dial in the mercury arm: the Wisdom of Veles, the serpent he strikes, is exactly what curbs the Excess. The old enemies balance each other — the thunderer's giving needs the serpent's knowing-when-to-stop.

variants.hexa_paint: In the paint palette Perun crowns the 12h arm in noon yellow — the pure high pigment of midday, the brightest color the wheel puts at the top. The thunder-mark's crown-petal glows the yellow of the sky at noon, Joy given the boldest hue on the dial, straight up where the light is highest.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette the top arm resolves to green — because green sits at the exact middle of the visible spectrum, and the middle of the spectrum belongs at the middle of the crown. Perun's noon becomes green here: the thunder-god wearing the balance-point of all light, the center wavelength raised to the zenith.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-pointed star the Sun claims the top, so Perun steps aside to the 9h arm in green — mid-morning rather than noon. The thunder-god yields the crown to Dažbog and takes the fresh green hour of late morning instead, the storm still gathering but the day still climbing.
variants.octa_light: In the octa light palette his mid-morning arm resolves to cyan — the cool blue-green of a clearing sky. Perun at 9h turns cyan here, the color of storm-light breaking, the thunder-god's hour caught in the bright wet blue-green that follows the rain.
variants.cross: The seasons cross keeps Perun at the 12h arm, colored yellow and aimed at the summer solstice — and he shares it with Dažbog, the sun body. The thunder-god and the giving sun stand together at the top of the year: high summer is the season of both the ripening light and the ripening storm, the sun and the rain crowning the dial as one.
variants.trio: In the trio Perun keeps the crown at 12h, now the Faith arm in Faith yellow, shared with Morana, the saturn body — and he guards the day third of the dial from 8h to 16h. The thunder-god at the top of the vertical holds the arm of Faith over the bright working hours, the storm-crown turned into the keystone of the day.

venus.base:
Mokoš is the great goddess — the only goddess Prince Vladimir set among the idols of his Kyiv pantheon, standing alone among the gods. She is the spinner: the flax and the wool, the distaff and the thread, and by extension the thread of fate itself, measured out and cut. And she is Moist Mother Earth, Mati Syra Zemlya, the damp fertile ground her very name seems to hold — mok-, the wet. When Christianity came she did not die but changed her name to St Paraskeva-Pyatnitsa, 'Friday,' venerated on the day that was already hers, where it stayed forbidden to spin.

The owner's medallion seats her as a veiled woman in a copper-bronze robe, a shawl over her long hair, haloed in a soft earth-green glow. In her raised hand she turns a distaff wound with pale flax; across her lap lies a curved harvest-sickle; and to either side of her stand full sheaves of ripe wheat. A serpent coils at her feet along the ground, and the whole field is the deep brown of turned earth — the goddess grown straight out of the soil she is.

Her day is Friday — petak in the Slavic count, 'the fifth,' but her true name for it is Pyatnitsa, the day of St Paraskeva, when no woman spun for fear of her. On the dial she takes the 20h arm, deep in the evening, in blood red, and her mood is Passion — the warm red heat of the evening hours, the fire of love and of the hearth after the day's work is done.

Her axis is Love against Jealousy. Mokoš is Love itself — the fertile, binding, life-spinning love of the earth-mother — but its shadow is Jealousy, the love that clenches into possession and fear of loss. Her cure is across the dial in the saturn arm: the Patience of Morana, the long slow patience of the ground that waits through winter, is what heals the Jealousy. Love that can wait does not need to grasp.

variants.hexa_paint: In the paint palette Mokoš holds the 20h arm in blood red — the deep mixed crimson of the evening, of the hearth-fire and the ripe fruit and the blood of birth. It is the warmest pigment on the lower dial, the color of Passion given body, the evening turned the red of love.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette her arm resolves to pure red — one of the three primary beams the white hub breaks into. Mokoš's evening becomes a fundamental ray: not the mixed crimson of the pigment but the clean elemental red that light itself is built from, Passion stripped to its root frequency.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-pointed star Mokoš moves up to the 18h dusk arm in glowing orange, and her mood shifts from Passion to Longing. The octa sets her exactly at sunset — the orange of the last light on the horizon, the goddess of the earth caught in the ache of dusk, love turned to Longing as the day lets go.
variants.octa_light: In the octa light palette the arm glows sunset orange — the exact color of the sky where the sun goes down. Mokoš at dusk wears the horizon's own hue, the Longing of the evening painted in the light of its going, warm and fading at once.
variants.cross: The seasons cross sets Mokoš on the 18h dusk arm, colored red and aimed at the autumn equinox — shared with Svetovid, the mars body. The earth-mother and the war-god stand together on the harvest arm: autumn is when the wheat she spins beside is cut and when the armies march, the sickle and the sword sharing one red dusk.
variants.trio: In the trio Mokoš keeps the 20h arm — and here the red Love arm is truly hers, the virtue that is her whole nature made into a direction. She shares it with Svetovid, the mars body: the earth-mother's Love and the war-god's guard on the same evening arm, the thing worth loving and the sword that keeps it.

saturn.base:
Morana is the goddess of winter and of death — Marzanna, Mara, Morena, her name rooted in the old word for death itself. She is the cold that kills the year, and every spring the Slavs kill her back: they build her as a straw effigy, dress her, carry her out of the village in procession, and drown or burn her at the vernal equinox to end the winter and let the spring in. The custom is still alive across the Slavic lands. She is death not as an end but as a hinge — the dying the world has to pass through before it can be born again.

The owner's medallion paints her as a gaunt, veiled figure on a winter throne, her death's face half-hidden, her tattered gray robes hung with icicles and rimed with frost. In one hand she lifts a staff crowned with a crescent moon, a small bound doll hanging from it; in the other she holds a poppy seed-pod, the sleep that shades into death. Bare dead trees and snow fill the field, a serpent lies coiled in the frost at her feet, and small straw effigies stand at her side — the very poppets that will be drowned for her. The border bristles with thistles, thorns and skulls.

Her day is Saturday — subota, the old Sabbath, the week's edge and its rest. Yet on the dial she takes the 8h arm in morning green, and her mood is Renewal — which for the death-goddess is the whole point. She sits at the hour of the week's rest and restart, the green of new morning, because the one who ends the year is also the one who clears the ground for it to begin again.

Her axis is Patience against Envy. Morana is Patience — the deep, frozen, waiting patience of winter and of the buried dead, the long stillness that outlasts everything — and her shadow is Envy, the cold resentment of the living and the warm. Her cure is across the dial in the venus arm: the Love of Mokoš, the warm earth-mother, is what dissolves the Envy. Winter's patience and spring's love are the same hinge seen from its two sides.

variants.hexa_paint: In the paint palette Morana holds the 8h arm in morning green (#007E00) — a mixed pigment, blue cooled into yellow, the green of new grass under the last frost. It is the strangest color on her: the death-goddess given the hue of first growth, Renewal painted in the green of the morning her winter finally releases.
variants.hexa_light: In the light palette her arm resolves to cyan (#00DDDD) — the cold bright blue-green of ice and thaw-water. Morana's morning turns to cyan here, the exact color of meltwater and winter light, the death-goddess wearing the hue of the very thaw that drowns her effigy.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-pointed star Morana moves to the 6h dawn arm in cyan (#00DCDC), and her mood shifts from Renewal to Hope. The octa sets her right at daybreak — the cold clear cyan of first light, the death-goddess standing at the dawn as Hope, the winter ending in the moment the sun clears the rim.
variants.octa_light: In the octa light palette her dawn arm resolves to a pale azure (#0080FF) — the clean cold blue of a clear morning sky. Morana at 6h turns the azure of the risen day, the frost burned off, the death-goddess dissolved into the plain bright light of the spring morning she makes possible.
variants.cross: The seasons cross moves Morana to the 6h arm, colored spring green and aimed straight at the vernal equinox — shared with Hors, the moon body. For her this is the most literal arm on the dial: the vernal equinox is the exact day her straw effigy is drowned. The death-goddess pointed at the very moment of her own drowning, standing on the hinge where winter dies into spring.
variants.trio: In the trio Morana is promoted all the way to the 12h crown, the Faith arm in Faith yellow (#F8E600), shared with Perun, the jupiter body. The death-goddess raised to the top of the star holds the arm of Faith beside the thunder-god — because the deepest Faith is the one that trusts the dying: that winter drowned is spring promised, the effigy in the river the year reborn.

sun.base:
Gold is the sun brought to earth: the one metal that never rusts, never tarnishes, never dulls — incorruptible, and therefore, to the alchemists, perfect. It is the goal of the whole Great Work, the magnum opus that climbs from black nigredo through white albedo and yellow citrinitas to red rubedo, and the sign for gold and the sign for the Sun are the same circle with a central point. Soft enough to beat into leaf a few atoms thick, dense enough to sink in any river, gold was money, crown, and reliquary at once — "as above, so below," the sun's own light made solid.

The owner's medallion casts the shared still life in pure gold: stacked ingots, a scatter of raw nuggets, and a coil of drawn wire, all resting inside a blazing solar corona that flares out behind them. The rim is a ring of sun rosettes — radiant starbursts and little sun-faces set in medallion circles — the emblem of the Sun repeated all the way round. It is the same wealth every one of the seven plates displays; here it is gold, the state the other six metals are always trying to become.

Sunday is the Sun's day in plain sight — Sunday, Sonntag, dies Solis — so gold takes the day and the very center of the dial. On the hexagram, the cross, and the trio it sits at the hub, the white-gold source from which all six colors of the star decompose, the way a prism unfolds one white beam into a spectrum. Only on the octa does it leave home, stepping up to the topmost 12h arm to blaze as noon itself.

Gold's virtue is Humility and its vice is Pride — the paradox of the perfect metal. The sun sits at the heart of everything yet asks for nothing; the danger is to mistake being the center for being above it. Pride is the false gold, all glare and no warmth; Humility is the true, the white light that gives itself to every color and keeps none. At the center, gold answers Pride not by dimming but by shining evenly on all.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment, gold holds the unmixable center. No two paints ever blend into white — white is where paint runs out — so the goldsmith does not mix it at all: he lays it on as leaf, beaten to gossamer and pressed whole onto the surface. The six colored arms radiate from a hub that can only be gilded, never ground. It is Humility as a technique — the one color you receive rather than make.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in prism light, the trick reverses. Here white is not the absence of color but the sum of all of it: the six colored beams run inward and meet at the hub, and where they overlap they add to pure white-gold light. The Sun is what the whole star adds up to. Gold, in light, is Humility rewarded — every arm gives its hue, and their gift, combined, is the source shining back.
variants.octa_paint: The eight-pointed star has no hub for a body to hold, so on the octa in pigment the Sun steps out of the center and climbs to the topmost 12h arm — noon itself, laid down in yellow. It is the one arrangement where gold stands in the ring with the others instead of beneath them, taking the crown of the star as plain noon-yellow leaf. Even risen to the top, its virtue is Humility: first among the arms, not above them.
variants.octa_light: On the octa in light, eight beams wheel around the hub and the Sun rides the topmost 12h point as pure emission — not the sum this time but the source, the white-gold spark the other seven catch and color. Noon is where the star pours out its light; the remaining arms are only that same light, bent. Gold at the top is the sun's own yellow-white fire, given freely to all eight directions.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons palette, four arms for the two solstices and two equinoxes — and gold keeps the crossing at their heart, the still point where all four seasons meet. The year turns around it: summer up, winter down, the equinoxes left and right, and the Sun at the axis that never moves. It is Humility as geometry — the center that lets everything else revolve, and takes no season for its own.
variants.trio: The trio is Faith, Hope and Love — three arms from a single root — and gold keeps the center where that root is anchored. From it the yellow Faith arm rises straight up the 12h line, with Hope at 4h and Love at 20h fanning below. The Sun is the source all three virtues draw from; Faith is simply gold pointing at noon. Humility holds the hub so that Faith, Hope and Love can each reach outward.

moon.base:
Silver is the moon's metal, the white counterpart to gold's yellow, and the alchemists gave it the albedo — the whitening, the second stage of the Work where the blackened prima materia is washed pure. Its sign is a crescent. Silver is the brightest reflector known, which is why every good mirror and every telescope's coating is silver; it throws back the light it is given rather than making its own, exactly as the Moon returns the Sun's. And silver tarnishes — it blackens in the air — so the metal itself keeps the moon's own habit of waxing bright and waning dark.

The owner's medallion renders the shared hoard in cool silver: the stacked bars, the raw nuggets, the coil of wire, lit by a soft lunar glow instead of a solar blaze. Around the rim runs a full sequence of crescent moons and phases — waxing and waning sickles set between star motifs — the Moon's emblem circling the plate the way it circles the month. Same still life, second metal: the wealth of gold shown again in its silver reflection.

Monday is the Moon's day and says so — Monday, Montag, lundi, lunes — so silver holds it. On the hexagram the Moon takes the 4h arm, deep blue, the last cold hour before dawn when the sky is darkest and the mood is Calm. The blue is the moon's night: not the fear of the dark but the quiet inside it. Of all the arms this is the one that waits — the hour that knows morning is coming and does not hurry it.

Silver's virtue is Serenity and its vice is Fear, the two faces of the dark. The same night that steadies one mind terrifies another; the mirror shows you whatever you bring to it. Fear is cured from across the dial by Courage on the Mars arm — the iron nerve that meets the ember afternoon — while Serenity is Fear already answered, the still blue that has made its peace with the hour before dawn. Silver keeps Calm by reflecting, never by resisting.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment, silver keeps the 4h arm in deep blue (#002FFF) — the darkest ground of the wheel, the hour before dawn mixed as a heavy night-blue. Paint reaches its coldest here; there is no bluer blue on the plate. It is Serenity ground into color: the calm of the last dark hour, laid thick where the star runs closest to midnight.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in prism light, the 4h arm burns as pure primary blue (#0040FF) — one of the three lights the whole spectrum is built from. In pigment blue is a mood; in light it is a foundation. The Moon becomes elemental here, a clean cold beam that the other hues need before they can make anything. Serenity, in light, is not dimness but a primary — the calm the rest of the star is composed from.
variants.octa_paint: On the octa in pigment the Moon slides to the 3h arm, a sea-deep blue (#005ADC) — an hour earlier, an hour deeper into the night. The eight-pointed star pushes her further from dawn, into the drowned blue of the small hours where the water and the sky are the same color. Calm turns oceanic here; Serenity is no longer waiting for morning but resting on the floor of the dark.
variants.octa_light: On the octa in light the arm resolves to pure blue (#0000FF), blue with nothing added — the absolute of the color, the deepest note the prism can strike. The Moon at her most elemental: not a shade of night but night's own frequency. Here Serenity is total, a blue with no Fear left in it because there is nothing else in it at all.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons, and it moves the Moon to the 6h arm — the spring equinox, colored spring green — and gives her a partner: she shares the arm with Lead, the Saturn metal. Silver and lead at the vernal balance, the brightest metal beside the dullest, the moon of the albedo standing where the year turns from dark to light. Serenity here is the calm of equinox, day and night in perfect equal measure.
variants.trio: On the trio the Moon holds her 4h arm, but it is now the Hope arm, colored Hope blue (#002FFF) — and she shares it with Quicksilver, the Mercury metal. Silver and quicksilver together on the arm of Hope: the two lunar-bright metals, one solid and one liquid, pointing the same blue at the coming dawn. Serenity and Hope are the same gesture here — the calm that waits is also the calm that expects.

mars.base:
Iron is the soldier's metal and the planet Mars made solid — the blade, the ploughshare, the nail, and the armor. It is the most useful metal humankind ever tamed: the Iron Age is named for it because working it changed the world. And Mars runs, quite literally, in our blood — the iron at the heart of every hemoglobin molecule is what binds oxygen and turns blood red, so the war-god's metal is the one we each carry, breath by breath, in our veins. Its sign is the shield-and-spear ♂, the same glyph the alchemists used for the metal and the astronomers for the red planet.

The owner's medallion beats the shared still life out of dark iron, and it is the only plate that arms itself: among the stacked bars, the raw ore and the coil of wire lie a long straight sword and a battered war-helmet, lit from behind by a smoldering red forge-glow. The rim is a soldier's rack — shields, crossed spears, swords, a helm, and the ♂ of Mars set at the crown. Same hoard as all the others, but here the wealth has been forged into weapons.

Tuesday is Mars' day — you hear it plainly in French mardi, from dies Martis — so iron takes it. On the hexagram Mars holds the 16h arm in ember orange, the working afternoon when the day is hottest at its labor and the mood is Zeal. It is the hour of effort, the anvil-hour, iron at the forge; orange is the color of the metal glowing just short of the point where it can be shaped. The afternoon belongs to the metal that gets things done.

Iron's virtue is Courage and its vice is Wrath — the sword that defends and the sword that murders, one blade, two hands. Zeal is the heat between them, and it can temper or it can burn. Wrath is quelled from across the dial by Serenity on the Moon's calm arm; Courage is Wrath with its edge turned outward, toward what must be faced rather than what one wants to destroy. Iron is only as good as the hand that holds it — the same metal for the shield and the axe.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment, Mars keeps the 16h arm in ember orange — iron in the forge, glowing at the exact heat where it yields to the hammer. Orange is the working color, the afternoon at its hottest labor, and in paint it sits warm and heavy between red and yellow. Zeal made visible: the arm of effort, the hour the metal is actually being made into something.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in prism light, orange has no seat — the light-wheel runs on primaries and their pure mixes — so Mars burns as pure yellow instead. It is the whetstone spark, the yellow-white flash of steel struck on stone. In light the ember cools to a blade's gleam; Zeal keeps its heat but trades the forge-glow for the edge's shine. The working metal, lit as the finished tool.
variants.octa_paint: On the octa in pigment Mars steps back to the 15h arm, a deep amber (#ECB800) — an hour earlier in the working afternoon, the color of iron cooling from orange toward gold. The eight-pointed star sets him mid-labor, past the peak heat but still at the anvil. Zeal in amber is endurance: the long middle of the work, when the first fury has settled into steady hammering.
variants.octa_light: On the octa in light the arm hardens to a brassy yellow, the sharp shine of metal already worked — filed, honed, and catching the sun on its new edge. This is not the forge but the finished blade; the yellow has gone metallic. Zeal here is the pride of the completed tool, iron that has passed through the fire and come out gleaming.
variants.cross: The cross gives Mars the 18h dusk arm — autumn, the autumnal equinox, colored dusk red — and he shares it with Copper, the Venus metal. Iron and copper on one arm: the two oldest working metals, the pair that Bronze-Age memory is built from, tools and blades from one and mirrors and coin from the other. Zeal at the equinox is the harvest's effort, the hardest labor of the year done in the reddening light.
variants.trio: On the trio Mars moves to the 20h arm — the red Love arm — standing beside Copper again and guarding the evening third of the day, the hours from 16h to 24h. Iron on the arm of Love is Courage in its oldest sense: to fight for something, not against it. Zeal turned toward what one protects rather than what one would break — the warrior keeping the evening watch.

mercury.base:
Quicksilver is the strangest of the seven — the only metal that is liquid at room temperature, a mirror you can pour, a silver that runs. The alchemists called it Mercurius and made it one of the tria prima, the three first principles, the very spirit of transformation: the mediator that dissolves gold and silver into itself and carries every metal from one state to another. It is the messenger between conditions, solid and liquid at once, and its sign ☿ — the crescent, circle and cross stacked together — marks the metal, the planet, and the quick god who runs between worlds.

The owner's medallion pours the shared still life in bright quicksilver, and it alone breaks the still: above the stacked bars, the nuggets and the coil of wire, a ribbon of liquid metal curls and beads across the top of the plate, refusing to stay solid. The whole surface has a pearled, mother-of-pearl iridescence, as if the metal could not decide on a single color. Round the rim run caducei, winged sandals, and the ☿ of Mercury — the emblems of the messenger, wings and serpents circling the one metal that will not hold a shape.

Wednesday is Mercury's day — French mercredi, dies Mercurii, says it outright — so quicksilver takes it, and takes the strangest seat on the dial: the bottom 0h/24h arm, midnight, mourning purple, mood Sorrow. It is the day's grave and its mystery, the deepest point of the clock where one day dies and the next is born. The messenger belongs at the threshold; the mediator between states sits exactly where midnight mediates between yesterday and tomorrow, in the purple of the deepest hour.

Quicksilver's virtue is Wisdom and its vice is Greed — the knowledge that transmutes and the hunger that hoards. Mercurius understands every metal because he can become each one; that is wisdom, the fluid mind that meets a thing on its own terms. Greed is cured from across the dial by Generosity on the Jupiter arm — the tin that gives its ring away — while Wisdom is Greed dissolved, the grip loosened into flow. The metal that will not be held teaches the mind not to clutch.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment, Quicksilver keeps the 0h arm at the very bottom, in mourning purple (#542E85) — the color of midnight and of the grave, mixed deep and cool. It is the lowest, quietest point of the star, the pigment of Sorrow. The messenger rests at the bottom of the dial where the day turns over, purple pooling at the hour that belongs to no day at all.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in prism light, the 0h arm lifts into magenta (#BD00BD) — the impossible color, the one hue with no single wavelength, made only where the spectrum's two ends bend around and meet. It is the perfect light for the mediator: a color that exists only as a bridge. Sorrow, in light, becomes the magenta seam where the ends of the rainbow are joined — quicksilver closing the circle at midnight.
variants.octa_paint: On the octa in pigment Quicksilver climbs to the 21h arm, a deep red (#DC0000) — late evening, hours before its midnight seat, the metal caught mid-journey up the western side of the dial. The eight-pointed star will not let the messenger settle; it moves him into the red of the closing night. Sorrow warms toward blood here, the restless mercury still running toward the bottom of the clock.
variants.octa_light: On the octa in light the 21h arm resolves to pure red (#FF0000) — the primary itself, the loudest note in the spectrum, strange clothing for the mourning metal. But the transformer wears every color in turn; tonight it is the reddest red. The messenger, lit as pure primary, is Sorrow with its volume turned all the way up — grief as a signal-fire on the late-evening arm.
variants.cross: The cross is the seasons, and Quicksilver keeps the 0h arm — the winter solstice, colored winter blue — and keeps it alone. Every other body shares its seasonal arm; the messenger has no partner. The metal that mediates between all the others holds the year's longest night entirely by itself, the single unshared season, the mercury that touches everything and belongs to no one. Sorrow, at the solstice, is solitude — and it suits him.
variants.trio: On the trio Quicksilver rises from the bottom to the 4h Hope arm, colored Hope blue (#002FFF), and shares it with Silver, the Moon metal. The two mirror-metals meet on the arm of Hope — one moon that reflects, one mercury that pours — both pointing their quicksilver at the coming dawn. The messenger leaves the grave of midnight for the hour before morning; Wisdom, on the Hope arm, is Sorrow that has learned to expect the light.

jupiter.base:
Tin is Jupiter's metal, the benevolent one, and its gifts are mostly gifts of sound and shine. Soft, silvery, and cheap enough to plate everything, tin is what makes bronze when you add it to copper and what makes bell-metal when you add more — the alloy that lets a cast bell ring true and an organ pipe hold its note. Bend a bar of pure tin and it complains: the famous "tin cry," the faint shriek of its crystals rearranging, a metal that literally speaks when you work it. Its sign ♃ is the same glyph for the metal, the planet, and the sky-father's thunderbolt.

The owner's medallion casts the shared still life in pale, soft-white tin — the stacked bars, the nuggets, the coil of wire, all in the mild pewter-bright tone that gives tin its modesty. The rim is Jupiter's own regalia: crowns and oak leaves alternating with sprays of lightning, and the ♃ glyph — drawn like a rounded "24" — set in medallions all the way round. The king's emblems around the humblest-looking metal: same wealth as the gold plate, dressed as tin, crowned all the same.

Thursday is Jupiter's day — French jeudi, dies Iovis — so tin takes it, and takes the crown of the star: the top 12h arm, noon yellow, mood Joy. It is the high point of the dial, midday at its brightest, and Jupiter the greater benefic sits there in gold-adjacent yellow, the most fortunate hour on the most fortunate arm. Where gold holds the center as source, tin holds the summit as celebration — the noon of the week, ringing like a struck bell.

Tin's virtue is Generosity and its vice is Excess — the open hand and the hand that never closes. Jupiter is expansion itself, and expansion is a blessing until it becomes bloat; the greater benefic gives, but too much of a good thing is still too much. Excess is curbed from across the dial by Wisdom on the Mercury arm — the quick mind that knows when to stop — while Generosity is Excess kept in measure, abundance that pours without flooding. Tin makes the bell ring; Wisdom keeps it from cracking.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment, Tin holds the 12h arm at the top in noon yellow — the crown of the star, midday laid down in warm pigment. It is the highest, brightest arm of the wheel, the color of the sun at its peak painted onto Jupiter's own summit. Joy made visible: the noon-yellow crest where the day is fullest and the metal rings its brightest. Generosity at the top of the dial, giving the whole afternoon its light.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in prism light, the 12h arm turns green — because green is the middle of the spectrum, and the middle belongs at the top, at noon. The most balanced light seats itself on the most fortunate arm. Joy, in light, is not the yellow of paint but the green at the spectrum's heart, the restful center-color crowning the star. Tin lit green is Generosity in equilibrium — abundance that has found its balance point.
variants.octa_paint: On the octa in pigment the Sun claims the topmost arm, so Tin steps aside to the 9h arm in green — mid-morning, the freshest quarter of the day. Displaced from the crown, Jupiter takes the growing hour instead, green as new leaves, the benefic presiding over the morning's climb. Generosity in mid-morning green is potential: the day still rising, the bell just beginning to ring.
variants.octa_light: On the octa in light the 9h arm cools to cyan — green shading toward blue, the clear light of mid-morning air. The eight-pointed star trades noon's crown for the fresh transparency of the growing day. Joy here is cyan-bright and rising, tin lit like dew on morning metal; Generosity as the day's first open hand, still unfolding.
variants.cross: The cross gives Tin the 12h arm — summer, the summer solstice, colored high yellow — and it shares that arm with Gold, the Sun metal. Tin beside gold at the year's peak: and this is exactly tin's genius, for in the alloys of bells and bronze, tin is what makes gold's neighbors ring. The humble metal stands at the summit beside the perfect one, not outshining it but making it sound. Joy at the solstice is the longest day, struck like a bell.
variants.trio: On the trio Tin keeps the 12h arm, now the Faith arm in Faith yellow, and shares it with Lead, the Saturn metal — the lightest useful metal beside the heaviest, both guarding the day's bright third from 8h to 16h. Tin and lead on the arm of Faith: the ringing metal and the silent one, holding noon together. Generosity here is the faith that gives before it is asked — the bell that rings for the whole working day.

venus.base:
Copper is the metal of Venus, and the connection is old and literal: the Romans got most of their copper from Cyprus, Venus' own island, and called it aes cyprium — "the metal of Cyprus" — which wore down over the centuries into cuprum, and then copper. The love-goddess's metal is the one named after her sea. Warm, red-gold, and endlessly workable, copper was the first metal humanity smelted and the mirror the ancient world polished to see itself; the very sign of Venus, ♀, is drawn as a hand-mirror, the circle of the glass above the cross of the handle.

The owner's medallion pours the shared still life in warm red-copper — the stacked bars, the nuggets, the coil of wire glowing in the metal's own rosy light — and adds one thing the other plates lack: a scatter of green gems tucked among the ore, copper's green heart showing through, the patina and the emerald both. The rim is entirely Venus: ♀ hand-mirrors, doves in flight, and scallop shells — the sea-shell she was born from — circling the plate. Same wealth, cast in the metal named for her island.

Friday is Venus' day — French vendredi, dies Veneris, keeps her name — so copper takes it. On the hexagram Venus holds the 20h arm in blood red, the evening, mood Passion. It is the warm hour after the work is done, the red of firelight and of the blood under the skin, the color the whole body wears when it is moved. Copper is the metal that blushes; the evening arm is where the day, its labor finished, finally feels.

Copper's virtue is Love and its vice is Jealousy — the open heart and the clutching one. Venus gives freely, but the same warmth that opens can also grasp; the mirror that shows the beloved can show a rival just as fast. Jealousy is healed from across the dial by Patience on the Saturn arm — the slow metal that can wait — while Love is Jealousy let go, the warmth that wants the other's joy more than its own possession. Copper greens with age, but the red beneath is still the metal that first learned to love.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment, Venus keeps the 20h arm in blood red — the evening laid down warm and deep, the color of firelight and of the blood the heart moves. Red is the most bodily pigment, and here it sits at the hour the day finally rests and feels. Passion made visible: the red arm of the evening, copper glowing at the temperature of the living pulse.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in prism light, the 20h arm burns pure red — the primary, the longest wavelength the eye can catch, the light that reaches furthest through the dusk. In pigment red is blood; in light it is the last color of the sunset, the one that lingers. Passion, in light, is that lingering red — Venus as the final warm beam before the dark, copper's evening fire made of pure emission.
variants.octa_paint: On the octa in pigment Venus slips to the 18h dusk arm in glowing orange, and her mood shifts from Passion to Longing — the exact hour of sunset, the ache of the light going. Orange is the color of the horizon at the moment it lets the sun go, and copper, of all metals, gleams most warmly there. Longing is Passion at a distance: the evening reaching after a day that is leaving.
variants.octa_light: On the octa in light the 18h arm glows sunset orange — copper's own true gleam, the exact warm shine of the polished metal catching low sun. This is the color copper was made to wear: not blood-red but burnished, the mirror-metal lit from the side at dusk. Longing here is luminous, the day's last warmth held in a metal that seems to keep the light a moment longer than it should.
variants.cross: The cross gives Venus the 18h arm — autumn, the autumnal equinox, colored dusk red — shared with Iron, the Mars metal. Copper beside iron at the equinox: the two Bronze-Age metals, the soft one and the hard one, mirror and blade, sharing the arm where the year tips into dark. Passion at the autumn balance is love in the reddening light, the warmth held steady as the days begin to shorten.
variants.trio: On the trio Venus keeps her 20h arm, and now it is the Love arm outright — colored Love red, and hers by right, though she shares it again with Iron. The evening arm belongs to the love-goddess; that Mars stands on it too only means Courage and Love keep the same watch. Passion and Love are one gesture here — copper on its own red arm, the metal named for Venus holding the hour named for what she is.

saturn.base:
Lead is Saturn's metal — the heaviest of the seven the alchemists knew, the dullest, the slowest to change, and therefore the beginning of everything. It is the prima materia, the base matter the whole Work starts from: black, dense, saturnine, the lead that alchemy promises to turn into gold. That single transformation — lead into gold, the lowest metal into the highest — is the entire dream of the art. Modern physics keeps the old symbolism honest: lead is so dense it stops radiation, the heavy metal that shields us from the invisible, still guarding as its planet always did. Its sign ♄ marks the metal, the ringed planet, and old Father Time.

The owner's medallion casts the shared still life in dark blue-grey lead — the stacked bars rough and unpolished, the nuggets, the coil of wire, and one heavy round lead disc, all under a cold dim light with none of gold's blaze. The rim tells you exactly whose metal this is: ♄ glyphs set in medallions and, between them, lengths of heavy chain linking the plate all the way round — Saturn's chains, the weight and the binding of the slowest planet. Same wealth as every other plate, shown in the metal it all begins as.

Saturday is Saturn's day and wears his name unmistakably — Saturday, dies Saturni — so lead takes it, the last day of the week and its rest. On the hexagram Saturn holds the 8h arm in morning green, mood Renewal — the week's rest and its restart, the quiet green hour when one cycle ends and the next has not yet begun. Lead is the metal of endings that are also beginnings; the prima materia waiting on Saturn's day for the Work to start again, green as the first growth after the fallow.

Lead's virtue is Patience and its vice is Envy — the slow trust and the sour comparison. Saturn is the planet of time, and time rewards patience and rots into envy; the same slowness that lets lead wait forever can curdle into resenting what came faster. Envy is dissolved from across the dial by Love on the Venus arm — the copper warmth that wants another's good — while Patience is Envy outlasted, the heavy metal content to be the base that gold is made from. Lead does not envy gold; lead knows it is where gold begins.

variants.hexa_paint: On the hexagram in pigment, Lead keeps the 8h arm in morning green (#007E00) — the quiet green of the week's restart, the fallow ground ready to grow. Green is the color of renewal in paint, and here it sits at the slow morning hour when the cycle turns over. Renewal made visible: the base metal at rest, green as the first shoot, patient on Saturn's own arm.
variants.hexa_light: On the hexagram in prism light, the 8h arm turns cyan (#00DDDD) — green cooled toward blue, the clear light of an early morning that has not yet warmed. In light, lead's renewal is not the green of growth but the cyan of dawn air, the hour before the day commits. Patience, lit, is that held breath of first light — the heavy metal waiting, cool and clear, for its turn to change.
variants.octa_paint: On the octa in pigment Lead moves down to the 6h dawn arm in cyan (#00DCDC), and its mood becomes Hope — and for lead, of all metals, this is the whole promise: the prima materia at the exact hour of dawn, the base matter waiting for the light that will begin its transmutation. The heaviest, slowest metal placed at daybreak. Hope here is literal — lead at the threshold of the gold it is meant to become.
variants.octa_light: On the octa in light the 6h arm opens to azure (#0080FF) — full daybreak, the cyan of dawn warmed into open morning sky. The eight-pointed star lifts lead out of the green of rest into the azure of the risen day. Hope, in light, is that widening sky — the prima materia no longer merely waiting but standing in the first real light, the Work about to begin.
variants.cross: The cross gives Lead the 6h arm — spring, the vernal equinox, colored spring green — shared with Silver, the Moon metal. Lead beside silver at the year's balance: the heaviest metal and the brightest, the prima materia and the albedo, standing together where the year turns toward light. Renewal at the equinox is the true restart, day and night made equal and the slow metal ready, green, for its long climb toward gold.
variants.trio: On the trio Lead is promoted all the way to the 12h Faith arm in Faith yellow (#F8E600), sharing the summit with Tin, the Jupiter metal — the heaviest metal raised to the top of the dial. It is the alchemist's whole hope drawn as geometry: lead lifted to noon, standing where gold's light is brightest, on the arm of Faith. Patience here is faith itself — the base matter trusting, at the crown of the star, that it will one day be gold.

Aries.base:
Aries opens the zodiac at the point astronomers still call the First Point of Aries, the spring equinox. On the year wheel it begins at 270 degrees, the left side of the dial, the year's own six in the morning, and guards 21 March to 19 April, when everything begins again.

The owner paints the ram rearing inside a dark disc, forelegs lifted in mid-leap, one horn wound into a full spiral, while the constellation is only a bent line of a few stars with one bright bend. So much force spent on so modest a piece of sky.

It rides the green arm of Renewal, Saturday's arm borrowed from Saturn, where the virtue is Patience and the shadow is Envy: a hard school for a headlong ram whose ruler Mars burns far away on the orange arm of Tuesday. Its arm-mate Taurus walks the same green path at a slower, steadier pace.

variants.paint: In the paint world Aries wears deep pigment green, the painter's mixture of yellow and blue. It is the green of first grass and wet spring earth, a color made of matter, and it grounds the ram: fire held inside growing things, impatience buried in a seed that must wait for its season.
variants.light: In the light world the spring arm glows cyan, green and blue light added together on the screen. The ram turns electric and cool, dawn air instead of soil, and its charge is no longer buried in earth but broadcast as pure signal, renewal read as energy rather than matter.
variants.paint_south: South of the equator the wheel turns and the ram leaps out of spring's green into the evening arm at 20h, wearing deep pigment red (#B60000), the hour whose mood is Passion. This is Venus's own arm, and the old story tells itself again in color: Mars's headlong sign lodged in the rooms of his mythic lover, Ares caught in Aphrodite's evening. The southern ram keeps its fire but points it at the heart instead of the season — impatience ripened into ardor, the charge of first grass become the charge of first love — and its shadow shifts with it, no longer Envy but that vice's hotter cousin Jealousy, passion that has begun to grip what it should only warm.
variants.light_south: In the southern light palette the ram's evening arm burns pure red (#FF0000), the first primary of the additive world. The cool cyan dawn of its northern reading is gone; what was buried charge is now emitted flame, the ram as beacon rather than seed. Courage broadcast as desire, at full brightness, in Venus's own hour — a signal that does not wait for its season but announces it.

Taurus.base:
Taurus is the bull of the oldest sky, remembered as the white shape Zeus wore to carry Europa across the sea. It guards 20 April to 20 May, the arc from 300 to 330 degrees, the heart of spring when the year climbs steadily toward its summit at the top of the dial.

The owner's bull stands inside a dark disc with its head raised and one foreleg lifted, horns curving upward, all chest and haunch. The constellation repeats those horns as a long open fork of stars, two bright tips diverging from the cluster at the bull's eye.

It stands on the green arm of Renewal, Saturday's arm under Saturn, teaching Patience against the vice of Envy, and no sign wears that lesson more naturally than the unhurried bull. Its ruler Venus keeps house on the red arm of Friday, and its arm-mate Aries supplies the spark that Taurus turns into steady ground.

variants.paint: In pigment Taurus wears deep green, yellow and blue ground together into the color of pasture and leaf. The subtractive world of matter is the bull's own element: color you can till, weigh and hold, renewal as something grown slowly out of soil rather than switched on.
variants.light: In the prism world the bull's arm shines cyan, blue and green light summed on the screen. The pasture becomes clear water and cold sky, and Taurus grows lighter than itself: the same patience, but read as a calm transparent frequency instead of heavy fertile earth.
variants.paint_south: South of the equator the wheel turns and the bull comes home: the mirrored year seats Taurus on the evening arm of Passion, Venus's own house, in pigment red (#B60000), an ox-blood crimson as heavy with earth as its northern green was with grass. The myth shifts weight along with the hue, for the shape Zeus wore to carry Europa was always a passion story, and the unhurried grazer becomes the steadfast lover: the same patience re-voiced as devotion, the old envy warming into the arm's own shadow, jealousy, love that holds on the way a bull holds ground.
variants.light_south: In the southern light palette the bull's evening arm blazes pure red (#FF0000), the additive primary, and the reading sharpens: no longer pasture or wine-dark clay but signal, desire announced at full brightness. Venus's homecoming beast keeps its slowness, but a slow fire is the longest-burning kind — passion as steady emission, devotion broadcast rather than buried in the field.

Gemini.base:
Gemini is Castor and Pollux, one twin mortal and one divine, and the myth turns on a gift: Pollux split his immortality so neither brother would be lost alone. The sky keeps them as two parallel chains of stars, two figures standing side by side.

The owner draws them as twin women in long gowns, hands joined between them, each raising a leafy sprig in her free hand, mirrored like a reflection. The glyph is two pillars bridged at top and bottom, the Roman two.

Gemini guards 21 May to 20 June, the arc from 330 degrees to the very top of the dial, spring ripening into summer. It sits on the yellow arm of Joy, Thursday's arm under Jupiter, whose virtue is Generosity and whose vice is Excess, the exact moral of its own myth. Cancer shares that arm just across the solstice, and Gemini's ruler Mercury lives on the purple arm of Wednesday.

variants.paint: In the paint world Gemini wears pure pigment yellow, a painter's primary that no mixing can produce. That suits the twins: something first-hand and unmixed, the brightest matter on the palette, joy laid on thick before any other color has touched it.
variants.light: In the light world the noon arm turns pure green, a primary of the prism as yellow is of the palette. Gemini keeps its rank but changes its voice: primary in both worlds, now glowing at the eye's most sensitive wavelength, quick conversation carried as living signal.
variants.paint_south: South of the equator the wheel turns and Gemini drops to the midnight arm, and the fall is a homecoming: the bottom of the dial is Wednesday's post, the house of its own ruler Mercury. Wearing purple (#542E85), red and blue pigment folded into the dye of mourning and of kings, the twins show the other face of their myth — Pollux's gift was also a grief, half of all their days spent below — and noon's quick talk deepens into the mood of Sorrow, wisdom traded in low voices, conversation kept like a vigil through the dark.
variants.light_south: In the light palette the southern midnight arm glows magenta (#BD00BD), red and blue light added into a color no single wavelength can carry. Nothing suits the twins better: two lights joined into one impossible hue, Castor and Pollux as pure signal, sorrow emitted rather than worn — the vigil turned luminous, midnight answering itself in light.

Cancer.base:
Cancer begins exactly at the top of the dial: its first point is the summer solstice, the anchor on which DOMY's whole year wheel is pinned, and the Tropic of Cancer still carries its name. It guards 21 June to 22 July, the first arc of high summer, 0 to 30 degrees.

The myth is small and stubborn: the crab Hera sent to pinch Heracles, crushed underfoot and set among the faintest stars of the zodiac, a dim slender Y in the sky. The owner restores its dignity, drawing the crab from above with both pincers raised open and eight legs fanned out in perfect symmetry.

It sits on the yellow arm of Joy, Thursday's arm under Jupiter, learning Generosity and shadowed by Excess, with Gemini as its neighbor on the same arm across the solstice. Its ruler is the Moon, at home on the blue arm of Monday: a child of night standing guard at the year's brightest gate.

variants.paint: In pigment Cancer wears primary yellow, the brightest matter a brush can carry. There is a tenderness in giving the Moon's shy creature the sunniest paint on the wheel: the crab keeps its shell, but Thursday's yellow warms it into open-handed, generous gold.
variants.light: In the light world Cancer's arm shines pure green light at the top of the star. The moonchild trades gold for the prism's own primary, and its softness turns luminous: not the glare of noon but the green of things that grow through it, joy as emitted light.
variants.paint_south: South of the equator the wheel turns and Cancer's solstice gate opens onto midwinter: the crab crosses to the bottom midnight arm and wears purple (#542E85), red and blue pigment folded into Mercury's dye of Sorrow. Thursday's open-handed gold is gone; here the Moon's shy creature learns the dark arm's curriculum, wisdom shadowed by greed, and its shell turns from ornament to shelter. It is the same stubborn guard Hera once sent, but re-tempered — pincers closed around what little warmth the long night allows, patient as tidewater, generous only after it has endured.
variants.light_south: In the southern light palette Cancer's midnight arm glows magenta (#BD00BD), the one hue no rainbow contains, conjured by the eye where the spectrum's far ends meet. Nothing suits the Moon's child better: moonlight too is borrowed radiance, and the crab becomes the lantern of the year's darkest gate, sorrow emitted rather than absorbed — a faint, invented shining that the sky cannot make and the mind refuses to put out.

Leo.base:
Leo is the Nemean lion, the first labor of Heracles, whose hide no weapon could pierce, set into the sky with Regulus, the little king, burning at its heart. It guards 23 July to 22 August, the arc from 30 to 60 degrees, the furnace weeks just past the solstice.

The owner's lion strides in profile, mane flaring like fire around its lifted head, tail carried in a long S with a tufted tip. The stars draw the same mane as the Sickle, a backwards question mark curling above the body.

It burns on the orange arm of Zeal, Tuesday's arm under Mars, where the virtue is Courage and the vice is Wrath, the lion's two temperatures. Virgo shares that arm as its quieter half. And Leo's ruler is unique: the Sun does not live on any arm but at the very center of the dial, the white light of Sunday from which all six arm colors are decomposed. The lion answers to the source itself.

variants.paint: In pigment Leo wears burnt orange, red and yellow milled together into the color of embers and late July. It is a mixed color for a proud sign, and that is its lesson: even the lion's fire is composed, courage blended from passion and joy, matter warmed rather than burning.
variants.light: In the light world Leo's arm blazes pure yellow, red and green light added until they sum toward the Sun's own brightness. Nothing is milled or muted here; the lion becomes a lamp, zeal as radiation, the closest of the six lights to the white center it answers to.
variants.paint_south: South of the equator the wheel turns and the lion crosses to the pre-dawn arm, trading its embers for blue (#002FFF), the deep worked pigment of the hour called Calm under the Moon. The hide no weapon could pierce becomes a night watchman's patience: Regulus, the little king, keeps the throne while its ruler the Sun is still below the horizon, courage cooled into composure, wrath banked to a guarded stillness, and the arm's own shadow, fear, faced down simply by staying.
variants.light_south: In the light palette the southern lion glows blue (#0040FF), the beam instead of the pigment. Regulus turns pilot light: the king's furnace reduced to one steady signal in the dark before dawn, zeal refined into vigil, a small unwavering lamp that answers fear with the promise of the Sun's return.

Virgo.base:
Virgo is the star-maiden, Astraea who left the earth last of the immortals, or Demeter of the grain; either way the sky agrees, hanging Spica, the ear of wheat, as the bright star in her hand. She guards 23 August to 22 September, from 60 degrees down to the autumn equinox at 90, closing the summer at the dial's right side, the year's dusk.

The owner draws her winged, head bowed so her long hair veils her face, a sprig held up in one hand, her gown falling in folds; the constellation sprawls wide around that bright hand-star.

She stands, surprisingly, on the orange arm of Zeal, Tuesday's arm under Mars, beside Leo, her louder arm-mate. The pairing is the lesson: the arm's virtue is Courage and its vice is Wrath, and Virgo shows courage in its harvest form, exact, patient and unarmed. Her ruler Mercury keeps its home on the purple arm of Wednesday.

variants.paint: In pigment Virgo wears harvest orange, red and yellow mixed into terracotta and ripe grain. The subtractive world suits her: a color of clay, bread and September fields, zeal made useful, the soldier's hue mellowed into everything the soldier fights to protect.
variants.light: In the light world her arm turns pure yellow, red and green light summed brighter than either. The gleaner becomes lucid: precision as illumination, the lamp on the desk rather than the fire in the field, her quiet zeal emitted steadily at the screen's warmest primary.
variants.paint_south: South of the equator the wheel turns and the maiden crosses to the Moon's pre-dawn arm, trading the soldier's orange for blue (#002FFF), the weighed-out lapis of the old workshops. The hour is Calm, and it reads her differently: zeal cools into serenity, her famous worry stands named at last as the arm's vice, Fear, and Mercury's lists are kept the water-bearer's way, a measured, quiet pouring in the dark. Astraea, last of the immortals to leave the earth, now holds the last watch before sunrise, Spica the one grain still bright at four o'clock.
variants.light_south: In the southern light palette her arm glows blue (#0040FF), a primary of both worlds, but here it is beam rather than pigment. The gleaner becomes the night's last lamp: precision as vigil, worry burned down to watchfulness, her exactness emitted steadily into the hour before dawn, serene because everything has already been counted.

Libra.base:
Libra is the only sign that is an object rather than a creature: a balance. It opens on 23 September at the autumn equinox, when day and night stand exactly equal and the year performs its own act of weighing, and it holds the wheel from 90 to 120 degrees, easing the light down into October.

The owner draws the idea honestly: a man on one knee, one arm raised, holding the two pans level from a single point of the beam. The glyph below is a sun resting on the horizon line, neither risen nor set.

It rides the red Friday arm of Venus, whose mood is Passion, whose virtue is Love and whose vice is Jealousy. Scorpio shares the arm with it, two schools of the same fire, one weighing passion and one plunging into it.
